proposals.title: Experimental and Mechanistic Modeling of Two-Phase Flow Regime Transitions in Inclined Tubes under Circumferentially Non-Uniform Heating
proposals.motivation: Advanced thermal management systems, such as passive cooling loops for servers and compact chemical reactors, increasingly rely on inclined two-phase flows for efficient heat transfer. However, existing flow pattern maps and heat transfer correlations are largely based on uniform heating or horizontal tubes, neglecting the critical coupling between circumferential wall temperature variation and buoyancy-driven secondary flows. This gap leads to inaccurate predictions of flow regime transitions and heat transfer coefficients, compromising the performance and reliability of high-heat-flux equipment.
proposals.hypothesis: Circumferential wall temperature variation in inclined tubes induces significant buoyancy-driven secondary flows that fundamentally alter two-phase flow pattern transitions and local heat transfer rates, creating a dynamic feedback loop not captured by conventional uniform-heating models.
proposals.method: We will construct an experimental facility using a transparent, electrically heated tube with segmented, independently controlled heaters to impose precise circumferential temperature profiles. High-speed imaging and local wall temperature sensors will map flow patterns and heat transfer for refrigerants (e.g., R134a, CO2) across inclinations. Data will inform the development of a 3D two-fluid CFD model in OpenFOAM, incorporating a modified momentum closure to account for thermally induced secondary flows. This model will be used to generate a new mechanistic flow pattern transition criterion based on local void fraction and circumferential Nusselt number distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first experimentally validated flow pattern map and set of local heat transfer correlations for inclined tubes under non-uniform heating. It will deliver an open-source, reduced-order model for predicting regime transitions, directly applicable to the design of next-generation thermosyphons, pulsating heat pipes, and compact two-phase heat exchangers.
proposals.novelty: The research uniquely captures the two-way coupling where the wall thermal boundary condition dictates the phase distribution, and the evolving flow pattern subsequently dictates the local circumferential heat transfer, a feedback mechanism absent from prior studies focused on either uniform heating or adiabatic flow.

proposals.title: A Predictive Framework for Forced Convection Response to Circumferentially Engineered Heat Flux: Experiments, Simulations, and Reduced-Order Modeling
proposals.motivation: Advanced thermal systems, from nuclear fuel bundles to high-flux electronics, often operate under spatially non-uniform heating, which can significantly alter flow structure and heat transfer compared to uniform boundary conditions. Pre-2026 design practices largely rely on empirical corrections or conservative uniform-heating assumptions, lacking a predictive framework for such complex scenarios. This gap limits the performance optimization and reliability of next-generation cooling systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The circumferential heat flux distribution in an internal flow can be mapped to specific, predictable modifications in the turbulent flow field, and these modifications can be captured by a reduced-order model correlating the Fourier modes of the imposed heat flux with key hydrodynamic and thermal response parameters.
proposals.method: A tunable test section with independently controlled, segmented heaters will generate a database of circumferentially non-uniform thermal boundary conditions under forced convection of a working fluid like CO2 or water. High-fidelity experimental data (local Nusselt numbers, wall temperatures, flow fields via PIV) will be acquired. Companion RANS (using k-omega SST with enhanced wall treatment) and wall-resolved LES simulations will be performed to isolate the governing physics. Data-driven techniques, specifically Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD) of the flow field and symbolic regression, will be used to develop the reduced-order model.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, physics-informed model that predicts flow and thermal performance for arbitrary circumferential heating patterns, moving beyond case studies. It will provide concrete design guidelines for tailoring heat flux distributions to enhance mixing or control wall temperatures in practical systems, directly impacting thermal management in servers, compact heat exchangers, and nuclear applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies describe specific non-uniform heating effects, this research program uniquely integrates a tunable experimental platform with high-fidelity simulations explicitly to develop a generalized predictive model. The focus on correlating the spectral content of the boundary condition with the flow response, rather than bulk parameters, represents a fundamental advance in convective heat transfer analysis.

proposals.title: Experimental and Numerical Investigation of Two-Phase Flow Regimes and Heat Transfer in a CO2 Thermosyphon under Circumferentially Non-Uniform Heat Flux
proposals.motivation: The assumption of uniform circumferential heat flux is common in the design of thermosyphons and heat pipes for electronics cooling and chemical reactors. However, real-world applications, such as server racks with asymmetric component layouts or methanation reactors with external heating jackets, impose highly non-uniform thermal boundary conditions. The impact of this non-uniformity on local flow patterns, dry-out conditions, and overall thermal performance remains poorly quantified, leading to potentially over- or under-designed systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that circumferentially varying heat flux will induce significant azimuthal variations in vapor quality and flow regime, leading to premature local dry-out and a measurable reduction in the overall heat transfer coefficient compared to predictions based on uniform flux models.
proposals.method: We will construct an instrumented, transparent thermosyphon/evaporator test section using CO2 as the working fluid, capable of applying independently controlled heat fluxes to four 90-degree circumferential segments. High-speed flow visualization will be synchronized with local wall temperature measurements from embedded thermocouples. The experimental data will be used to validate a 3D CFD model using the Volume of Fluid (VOF) method in ANSYS Fluent (pre-2026 version), incorporating user-defined functions to apply the non-uniform heat flux boundary condition and a suitable boiling model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed map of two-phase flow regimes and local heat transfer coefficients for a thermosyphon under controlled non-uniform heating. It will yield a new correlation or correction factor for design models, enabling more accurate performance prediction for asymmetrically heated passive cooling systems in electronics and process industries.
proposals.novelty: While non-uniform axial heating has been studied, the explicit focus on circumferential non-uniformity in a closed-loop thermosyphon, combined with synchronized high-speed visualization and 3D CFD validation for CO2, represents a significant advance beyond the standard uniform-heat-flux paradigm dominant in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Unified Model for Heat-Flux-Dependent Two-Phase Flow Pattern Transitions on Engineered Surfaces
proposals.motivation: The design of high-performance passive cooling systems, such as thermosyphons and pulsating heat pipes for electronics and reactors, relies on accurate prediction of two-phase flow regimes. Pre-2026 work treats surface properties (roughness, wettability) and heat flux as independent variables, yet in high-flux applications, the intense boiling activity dynamically alters the effective wall conditions. This gap leads to suboptimal thermal management and instability in next-generation cooling devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applied heat flux non-linearly modulates the effective surface characteristics (e.g., dynamic contact angle, active nucleation site density) experienced by a two-phase flow, thereby fundamentally reshaping flow pattern transition boundaries rather than merely shifting them along pre-existing maps.
proposals.method: The research will employ an experimental closed-loop facility using CO2 as the working fluid in a transparent macro-channel test section. Engineered surfaces with controlled roughness (via micromachining) and wettability (via coatings) will be fabricated. High-speed flow visualization synchronized with local heat flux and temperature measurements will capture flow pattern transitions under varying heat fluxes. Data will be used to develop a semi-mechanistic model where transition criteria (e.g., from bubbly to slug flow) are functions of a coupled parameter integrating static surface morphology and a flux-dependent thermal boundary condition.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new predictive framework for two-phase flow stability in passive cooling systems, directly informing the design of advanced thermosyphons and heat pipes. It will provide engineers with a tool to co-optimize surface engineering and operational heat loads for enhanced thermal performance and reliability.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond correlating independent effects to explicitly modeling the inseparable, synergistic feedback loop between static surface properties and dynamic, flux-driven thermal-hydraulic wall conditions—a coupling largely overlooked in pre-2026 flow pattern maps.

proposals.title: Experimental Validation and Extension of a Universal Two-Phase Flow Pattern Map to Water, Ammonia, and Water-Al2O3 Nanofluids for Passive Cooling Applications
proposals.motivation: Flow pattern maps are critical for predicting heat transfer and pressure drop in two-phase cooling systems, but existing maps are largely developed and validated for refrigerants. The growing demand for high-performance, passive cooling in electronics and energy systems (e.g., servers, reactors) necessitates the use of alternative fluids like water (high latent heat), ammonia (excellent transport properties), and nanofluids (potential heat transfer enhancement). A validated, generalized map for these fluids is lacking, hindering the optimized design of advanced thermosyphons and pulsating heat pipes.
proposals.hypothesis: A flow pattern map developed for refrigerants, when parameterized using appropriate dimensionless numbers (e.g., Reynolds, Weber, Bond), can be successfully extended to predict flow regimes for water, ammonia, and nanofluids in mini/macro-channels under conditions relevant to passive cooling systems.
proposals.method: The research will employ an experimental closed-loop facility with a transparent test section (macro- and mini-channel scales) for flow visualization. Pre-2026 established flow pattern identification algorithms (e.g., via image processing of high-speed video data using grayscale intensity analysis and pattern recognition) will be used to classify regimes (slug, annular, etc.). Systematic experiments will measure flow patterns, heat transfer coefficients, and pressure drops for water, ammonia, and a water-Al2O3 nanofluid across a range of heat fluxes, mass fluxes, and vapor qualities. The data will be used to test and adapt a pre-existing dimensionless map (e.g., based on the work of Thome et al. for CO2) by comparing predicted versus observed flow transitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive experimental dataset and a validated, generalized flow pattern prediction tool for key non-refrigerant working fluids. It will directly enable more accurate thermal modeling and design of high-heat-flux cooling systems using thermosyphons and pulsating heat pipes. The results will clarify the efficacy of nanofluids in modifying two-phase flow regimes and associated heat transfer.
proposals.novelty: While flow maps exist for many refrigerants and CO2, their explicit validation and necessary adaptation for water, ammonia, and nanofluids in the context of passive, high-heat-flux cooling architectures is novel. The concurrent study of these three distinct fluid classes within a unified mapping framework to serve electronics and reactor cooling is a significant advancement.

proposals.title: A Mechanistic Criterion for Condensate Film Dryout Onset in Micro-Channels: Integration of Interfacial Shear and Wall Adhesion Effects
proposals.motivation: In high-performance passive cooling systems like thermosyphons and pulsating heat pipes, efficient condensation is critical. The onset of dryout during condensation drastically reduces heat transfer coefficients and can lead to device failure. Existing models, largely empirical, fail to reliably predict dryout onset under the complex interfacial shear and mass flux conditions prevalent in compact cooling applications, limiting design optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: The onset of condensation dryout is governed by a local force balance where the interfacial shear stress overcomes the combined effects of liquid film surface tension (adhesion) and viscous forces, and this threshold can be characterized by a modified dimensionless group integrating these parameters.
proposals.method: The research will employ an integrated experimental and analytical modeling approach. Experiments will use a purpose-built, optically accessible condensation test section, with CO2 and low-GWP refrigerants as working fluids, to visualize dryout onset in single micro-channels under controlled pressure, mass flux, and vapor quality. High-speed imaging and infrared thermography will capture film dynamics and local heat flux. The analytical model will be developed by extending a steady-state, one-dimensional two-fluid model framework. A force balance at the liquid-vapor interface will be formulated, and machine learning (specifically, a gradient-boosted regression tree algorithm) will be used to correlate the experimental dryout data and identify the dominant dimensionless parameters (e.g., a modified Weber number or Martinelli parameter) that define the critical condition.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, physics-based predictive criterion for condensation dryout onset, directly applicable to the design of next-generation passive cooling systems for electronics and reactors. It will advance fundamental understanding of two-phase flow stability limits during condensation, bridging a key gap between boiling dryout research and condensation phenomena.
proposals.novelty: While dryout during flow boiling has been extensively studied, a mechanistic criterion for condensation dryout, particularly one that explicitly integrates the competing effects of interfacial shear and wall adhesion forces in micro-channels, represents a significant and underexplored advancement in two-phase heat transfer.

proposals.title: Active Feedback Control of Annular Flow Stability in Microchannel Evaporators Using Localized Thermal Perturbations
proposals.motivation: The thermal management of high-power-density electronics and compact energy systems is increasingly reliant on flow boiling in microchannels. However, the performance of these systems is fundamentally limited by the onset of dryout, a critical heat transfer deterioration event triggered by flow regime transitions. Current approaches focus on passive geometry optimization or predictive models, but they cannot adapt to dynamic thermal loads or inherent flow instabilities, leaving a significant performance and reliability gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transition from a stable, high-performance annular flow to a dryout-prone regime (e.g., mist or stratified flow) in a horizontal microchannel can be actively delayed by applying real-time, localized thermal perturbations. This targeted actuation will modify local interfacial forces and vapor generation to stabilize the liquid film and suppress dryout inception.
proposals.method: The research will employ an experimental closed-loop with a horizontal microchannel evaporator using a low-GWP refrigerant (R1234ze(E) or CO2). Local flow regimes will be detected in real-time using synchronized high-speed visualization and impedance sensors. A control algorithm, based on a Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) framework with gain scheduling tuned to identified flow regime features, will command an array of microscale thin-film heaters patterned on the channel's exterior. These actuators will deliver precise, localized heat pulses to perturb the liquid-vapor interface and maintain annular flow stability under varying heat flux and mass flux conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-of-its-kind experimental demonstration of active dryout delay via direct flow regime control, moving beyond passive design. It will produce a validated control strategy and a new dataset linking actuation parameters to flow pattern stability, directly informing the design of next-generation adaptive thermal management systems for servers and power electronics.
proposals.novelty: While sensing of two-phase flow regimes exists, the closed-loop, active control of these regimes using localized thermal actuation to specifically target dryout mitigation is a novel paradigm. This approach treats the flow pattern as a controllable state variable, diverging from conventional passive thermal design.

proposals.title: Experimental Investigation of Flow Pattern Transitions and Dryout Characteristics of Low-GWP Refrigerants in Microscale Horizontal Tubes for High-Heat-Flux Electronics Cooling
proposals.motivation: The drive for compact, high-performance electronics and server cooling necessitates efficient microscale two-phase systems. However, predictive models for flow patterns and the critical dryout condition in horizontal microchannels (Dh < 1 mm) remain unreliable, especially for next-generation low-GWP refrigerants like R1234ze(E) and R1234yf. This knowledge gap impedes the design of reliable, passive cooling solutions such as advanced thermosyphons and micro pulsating heat pipes for data centers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for horizontal microchannels, the dominant flow pattern transition boundaries and the dryout heat flux for low-GWP refrigerants are governed by a modified dimensionless scaling that integrates the Boiling number, Confinement number, and a dynamic Weber number, deviating significantly from macro-scale correlations.
proposals.method: The experimental approach utilizes a closed-loop flow boiling rig with precise temperature, pressure, and mass flow control. A transparent, electrically heated sapphire test section (horizontal, inner diameter 0.5 mm and 1.0 mm) will enable simultaneous high-speed flow visualization and local heat transfer measurement. Refrigerants R134a (baseline), R1234ze(E), and R1234yf will be tested across a matrix of mass fluxes (50-500 kg/m²s), heat fluxes (up to 200 kW/m²), and vapor qualities. Data will be analyzed to map flow regimes (annular, slug, churn) and identify dryout inception, with results used to develop and validate a new semi-empirical model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unique, high-fidelity dataset mapping flow patterns and dryout limits for modern refrigerants in horizontal microchannels. It will yield a new predictive correlation for dryout heat flux, directly applicable to the thermal design of microchannel evaporators in passive two-phase cooling systems for high-heat-flux electronics and servers.
proposals.novelty: While microchannel flow boiling has been studied, the combined focus on horizontal orientation, low-GWP refrigerants, and the explicit link between visualized flow pattern dynamics and the dryout mechanism for passive cooling applications is novel. This integrates Dr. Thome's expertise in CO2 microchannels and thermosyphon/pulsating heat pipe systems into a new, fundamental experimental study.

proposals.title: Validation and Adaptation of Macro-Channel Pressure Drop Correlations for CO2 Flow Boiling in Micro-Channel Evaporators
proposals.motivation: The design of efficient, compact cooling systems for high-heat-flux electronics and server racks increasingly relies on CO2 flow boiling in micro-channel evaporators due to its excellent thermophysical properties. However, accurate pressure drop prediction in micro-channels remains a critical challenge; methods validated for macro-channels often fail as surface tension and viscous forces dominate, leading to suboptimal system performance and reliability. Extending validation to micro-geometries is essential for developing reliable design tools for next-generation passive and pumped two-phase cooling systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the most accurate macro-channel pressure drop correlations for CO2 can be adapted for micro-channels by incorporating a dimensionless scaling parameter that accounts for the increased influence of surface tension and flow confinement. This adapted model will predict experimental micro-channel pressure drop data within an acceptable error band (±20%).
proposals.method: The research will employ an experimental-computational approach. First, flow boiling pressure drop data for CO2 will be collected using a dedicated test facility with micro-channel test sections (hydraulic diameters 0.5-1.0 mm), varying mass flux, heat flux, and saturation temperature. Concurrently, leading macro-channel correlations (e.g., Müller-Steinhagen and Heck, Friedel) will be evaluated against this dataset. The best-performing macro method will be adapted by introducing a confinement number (Co) or a modified two-phase multiplier into its structure. The adaptation will be optimized using a non-linear regression algorithm to minimize the mean absolute error against the experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical pressure drop prediction method specifically for CO2 in micro-channels, directly addressing a key design uncertainty. It will contribute a new, adapted correlation or a modification factor to the literature, enabling more accurate thermal-hydraulic design of micro-evaporators for electronics cooling and compact heat exchangers. The results will strengthen the foundational engineering data needed for scaling passive thermosyphon and pumped systems using CO2.
proposals.novelty: While macro-channel correlations for CO2 are established, their systematic validation and physics-based adaptation for micro-channels, explicitly for CO2, is not comprehensively documented. The novelty lies in the targeted modification of a macro-scale method using a confinement-based scaling parameter derived from experimental micro-channel data, creating a bridge between well-understood macro-scale phenomena and micro-scale application needs.

proposals.title: Transient Two-Phase Pressure Drop Characteristics of CO2 in a Thermosiphon Loop for Server Cooling: Experimental Investigation and Model Development
proposals.motivation: The adoption of CO2 in passive cooling systems like thermosiphons for high-density server racks is promising due to its favorable thermophysical properties. However, existing design guidelines are based on steady-state pressure drop correlations. The dynamic pressure gradients during startup, shutdown, and load transients are poorly understood, posing a risk of flow instability, maldistribution, and thermal stress in practical, intermittently operated systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transient two-phase pressure drop of CO2 in a thermosiphon evaporator will significantly exceed steady-state predictions during rapid load changes due to delayed phase distribution and flow regime transitions, and that this overshoot can be characterized by a dimensionless time constant related to the system's thermal inertia.
proposals.method: We will construct an instrumented, closed-loop CO2 thermosiphon with a representative micro-channel evaporator plate, mimicking a server cooling module. A programmable electrical heater will impose controlled, stepwise and ramped heat loads. High-frequency pressure transducers and differential pressure cells will capture transient pressure gradients. The experimental data will be used to develop and validate a one-dimensional transient model, integrating existing drift-flux correlations with a time-dependent void fraction model solved using an explicit finite-difference scheme.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated dataset and a semi-empirical modeling framework for transient CO2 two-phase pressure drop in thermosiphons. It will deliver practical design guidelines for managing transient operations in CO2-based passive cooling systems, enhancing their reliability for electronic cooling applications.
proposals.novelty: While steady-state CO2 flow boiling is well-studied, this work uniquely focuses on the *transient* hydraulic response within a passive thermosiphon architecture, linking dynamic thermal loads directly to two-phase pressure drop evolution—a critical gap for real-world, non-steady operation.

proposals.title: Experimental Validation and Extension of a Two-Phase Flow Pattern Map for Low-GWP Refrigerants in Micro/Mini-Channels
proposals.motivation: The phase-out of high-GWP refrigerants like R134a and R404A is driving rapid adoption of low-GWP alternatives such as R1234yf, R1234ze(E), and R32. Accurate two-phase flow pattern maps are critical for designing efficient evaporators in cooling systems, but existing maps are largely based on older fluids and may not predict the flow regimes of these new refrigerants accurately, especially in the micro/mini-channels relevant for compact heat exchangers and electronics cooling.
proposals.hypothesis: A recently proposed generalized flow pattern map, developed from data for traditional refrigerants like CO2 and R134a, can be successfully extended to predict flow regimes for newer low-GWP refrigerants across a wide range of operational conditions (mass flux, heat flux, saturation temperature, channel diameter) if fluid-specific properties are adequately accounted for.
proposals.method: The research will employ an experimental closed-loop facility with a transparent test section (horizontal, circular, diameters from 0.5 mm to 3 mm) for flow visualization. We will systematically test refrigerants R1234yf, R1234ze(E), R32, and a baseline (R134a) across mass fluxes (50-800 kg/m²s), heat fluxes (5-50 kW/m²), and saturation temperatures (5°C to 25°C). High-speed imaging will be used to identify flow patterns (annular, slug, bubbly, intermittent). The observed transitions will be compared to predictions from the target map, and dimensionless parameters (e.g., Weber, Froude numbers) will be analyzed to propose refinements to the map's transition criteria.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, experimentally validated database of flow patterns for key low-GWP refrigerants. It will yield a refined and more universally applicable flow pattern map, directly aiding in the thermal-hydraulic design of next-generation evaporators for high-performance cooling systems, including server cooling and heat pump applications.
proposals.novelty: While flow maps exist for common refrigerants, this study provides the first comprehensive validation and necessary modification of a generalized map specifically for the newer class of low-GWP fluids under conditions relevant to modern, compact cooling technologies, bridging a critical gap between fundamental research and applied engineering design.

proposals.title: Active Heat Flux Modulation for Flow Pattern Control and Stability Enhancement in a CO2 Thermosyphon for Server Cooling
proposals.motivation: The thermal management of high-power-density electronic equipment, such as servers, increasingly relies on passive two-phase cooling systems like thermosyphons for efficiency. However, these systems can suffer from flow instabilities and dryout, which degrade heat transfer performance and reliability. Pre-2026 research on CO2 flow boiling, while extensive, has largely focused on passive channel design rather than active, real-time control of the two-phase flow regime to maintain optimal conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: That deliberate, high-frequency modulation of the applied heat flux at the evaporator can actively manipulate the prevailing two-phase flow pattern (e.g., from churn to annular flow), thereby suppressing flow instabilities, delaying dryout, and enhancing overall heat transfer coefficient and stability in a CO2 thermosyphon.
proposals.method: We will construct a closed-loop, mini-scale thermosyphon test rig with a transparent macro-channel evaporator section, using CO2 as the working fluid. A high-speed camera and pressure sensors will characterize the baseline flow patterns and instabilities. An active control system will be implemented, where a PID controller, using real-time wall temperature or pressure drop as the input, modulates the power to segmented heaters at frequencies (1-10 Hz) designed to interact with the bubble departure and coalescence dynamics. The algorithm will aim to maintain a stable annular flow regime by applying corrective heat flux pulses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel control strategy for two-phase passive cooling systems, directly linking active thermal input to flow pattern hydrodynamics. It will yield quantitative data on the stability envelope and heat transfer enhancement achievable via active modulation, offering a new pathway to improve the performance and reliability of server cooling systems beyond passive design limits.
proposals.novelty: While heat flux is typically a system input to be dissipated, this proposal treats it as an active control variable to manipulate the internal two-phase flow hydrodynamics directly, creating a dual problem of heat removal and flow pattern stabilization—a concept not explored in pre-2026 thermosyphon or CO2 boiling literature.

proposals.title: Experimental Assessment of Al2O3-Water Nanofluids in a CO2 Thermosyphon for High-Flux Server Cooling
proposals.motivation: The increasing heat flux in high-performance computing and power electronics demands advanced passive cooling solutions. Thermosyphons and pulsating heat pipes offer efficient, passive heat transport, but their performance is limited by the thermophysical properties of conventional working fluids like CO2 or water. Nanofluids, engineered suspensions of nanoparticles in base fluids, present a pre-2026 opportunity to enhance boiling heat transfer and thermal conductivity in these systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The addition of low-concentration (≤1.0 wt%) alumina (Al2O3) nanoparticles to a water-filled thermosyphon will significantly enhance its heat transfer coefficient and critical heat flux during flow boiling, compared to pure water, by modifying nucleation site density and bubble dynamics.
proposals.method: The research will design and construct a closed-loop, visualizable thermosyphon test rig with a macro-channel evaporator section, instrumented for local heat flux and temperature measurement. Stable Al2O3-water nanofluids at concentrations of 0.1%, 0.5%, and 1.0% will be prepared using a two-step method with pH control and ultrasonication. Experiments will systematically compare the thermal-hydraulic performance (heat transfer coefficient, pressure drop, flow regime via high-speed imaging) of nanofluids against pure water and baseline CO2 data, under varying heat fluxes (up to 200 kW/m²) and filling ratios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide quantitative, benchmark data on nanofluid performance in a passive thermosyphon geometry directly relevant to electronics cooling. It will elucidate the trade-offs between heat transfer enhancement and potential stability or pumping power penalties, offering practical guidelines for integrating nanofluids into next-generation cooling systems for servers and power electronics.
proposals.novelty: While nanofluids have been studied in simple geometries, their application within the specific, gravity-driven two-phase flow regime of a thermosyphon—a core technology in the researcher's portfolio—for server cooling is novel. This bridges the gap between nanofluid fundamentals and deployable passive cooling system design.

proposals.title: Inverse Mapping of Circumferential Heat Flux via Convolutional Neural Networks and Two-Phase Flow Regime Recognition
proposals.motivation: Advanced thermal management systems, from electronics cooling to chemical reactors, increasingly rely on non-uniform heating to optimize performance and avoid dryout. However, directly measuring the circumferential heat flux distribution in an operating two-phase system is extremely difficult. Existing flow regime maps and thermal models are largely forward-looking and cannot infer the causative heating pattern from observable system states, limiting active control strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: That a machine learning model, trained on high-fidelity simulation data, can learn a robust inverse mapping between measurable two-phase flow parameters (e.g., local temperature, pressure drop, void fraction) and the circumferential wall temperature or heat flux distribution that produced them, enabling simultaneous flow regime classification and heat flux estimation.
proposals.method: We will generate a high-fidelity dataset using validated CFD simulations (e.g., VOF model in ANSYS Fluent) of CO2 flow boiling in a horizontal tube under diverse, parametrized non-uniform heating conditions. A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture will be designed to process spatially-distributed sensor data (simulated as circumferential temperature arrays) as input images. This CNN will be trained to perform a dual-output regression (inferred heat flux profile) and classification (flow regime, e.g., stratified, annular, slug). The model will be validated against experimental data from an instrumented thermosyphon or mini-channel test rig.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, data-driven inverse heat transfer model for two-phase systems, moving beyond passive diagnostics to an active inference tool. It will provide a framework for predictive thermal control in high-heat-flux equipment, allowing systems to be tuned in real-time to achieve target thermal performance or avoid critical heat flux based on inferred conditions.
proposals.novelty: While ML for flow regime recognition exists, its fusion with inverse heat conduction modeling to deduce a causative, spatially-complex boundary condition from within the flow is a significant advancement. This shifts the paradigm from classifying an observed state to actively diagnosing the thermal driver of that state.

proposals.title: Mapping Flow Regime Transitions in Two-Phase Systems Under Spatiotemporally Programmed Heat Flux
proposals.motivation: Advanced cooling systems for electronics and reactors increasingly rely on two-phase convective flows, where performance is critically tied to flow regime. Existing transition criteria (e.g., for laminar-turbulent or bubbly-slug flow) assume uniform heat flux, a condition rarely met in real applications like server hotspots or methanation reactors. This gap leads to suboptimal or unstable thermal management. A predictive model linking flux distribution to regime transitions is essential for designing next-generation passive and active cooling systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transition between convective flow regimes is governed not only by the total heat input but by the spatial wavelength and temporal frequency of the applied heat flux. Specifically, we posit that a dimensionless group combining a flux non-uniformity parameter with traditional Reynolds and Jakob numbers can collapse transition data across different flux patterns.
proposals.method: We will construct an experimental test facility using a transparent macro-channel evaporator with a programmable, tile-based heater array to impose precise 2D heat flux patterns (stripes, checkerboards, moving spots). Refrigerant R134a and CO2 will be used as working fluids. High-speed flow visualization and local temperature/pressure measurements will identify regime transitions. Concurrently, we will perform 3D numerical simulations using the Volume of Fluid (VOF) method in OpenFOAM, implementing user-defined functions for the spatiotemporal heat flux boundary conditions to generate detailed flow maps and validate the experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first generalized scaling law that quantifies how spatiotemporal heat flux variations shift flow regime maps in convective systems. It will deliver a validated dataset and correlation for engineers to predict operational stability in non-uniformly heated cooling devices. The results will directly inform the design of high-performance thermosyphons and pulsating heat pipes for electronics and chemical reactors.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined uniform or transient heat loads, this research systematically decouples the effects of total power from its spatial pattern and temporal dynamics on flow regime transitions, a factor largely overlooked in existing two-phase flow models. The use of a programmable heater as an 'adversarial' thermal boundary condition to probe transition stability is a novel experimental paradigm.

proposals.title: Coupled Flow Regime and Heat Flux Co-evolution in a Non-Uniformly Heated Two-Phase Thermosyphon: An Experimental and Mechanistic Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: Advanced cooling systems for high-power electronics and reactors increasingly rely on passive two-phase loops like thermosyphons and pulsating heat pipes. Design tools use flow pattern maps derived under uniform or adiabatic conditions, but real systems experience highly non-uniform heat loads (e.g., server hotspots). The assumption of decoupled flow patterns and heating is untested and potentially invalid, risking significant performance misprediction and system failure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a non-uniformly heated two-phase system, the local flow regime and the effective local heat flux are dynamically coupled, creating a co-evolutionary system where classical, boundary-condition-independent flow pattern maps do not hold.
proposals.method: We will construct an instrumented, variable-geometry thermosyphon test rig using a low-GWP refrigerant (e.g., R1234ze(E) or CO2). A segmented, independently controlled heater array will impose programmable non-uniform axial heat flux profiles. High-speed flow visualization and local heat transfer coefficient measurements will be synchronized. Data will be used to develop and validate a 1D transient model that couples a drift-flux formulation with local energy balances, explicitly solving for the feedback between regime-dependent heat transfer and the evolving thermal boundary condition.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first experimentally validated framework for predicting flow regime transitions under non-uniform heating. It will deliver a new class of coupled flow pattern/heat transfer correlations and a predictive model, directly improving the design accuracy of passive cooling systems for data centers and chemical reactors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating the flow pattern map not as a fixed input but as a dynamic state variable that co-evolves with the thermal field, challenging a foundational assumption in two-phase flow science that has persisted since the 1970s.

proposals.title: A Physics-Based Modular Framework for Predicting Flow Boiling Heat Transfer in Enhanced Surface Tubes: From Feature-Induced Mechanisms to Generalized Design
proposals.motivation: Enhanced surface tubes are critical for improving the performance of evaporators in cooling systems, including those for electronics and chemical reactors. However, current design relies heavily on empirical correlations for specific geometries, limiting predictive capability for new fluids or feature combinations. A mechanistic understanding of how surface features like fins, dimples, or grooves influence fundamental two-phase flow phenomena is needed for rational, generalized design.
proposals.hypothesis: The heat transfer enhancement in structured tubes can be predicted by a modular model that superposes the contributions of discrete, physics-based mechanisms (e.g., vortex-induced thin-film evaporation, nucleation site density modulation, and convective liquid renewal) triggered by specific surface feature geometries.
proposals.method: The research will combine targeted experimentation with model development. A test facility will be built for flow boiling of refrigerants (e.g., R134a, CO2) and water in tubes with systematically varied internal features. High-speed flow visualization and infrared thermography will map local flow regimes and heat transfer coefficients to isolate mechanism contributions. A modular computational framework will be developed, where each module represents a physical mechanism (e.g., a sub-model for vortex shedding frequency and its impact on local heat transfer), parameterized by feature geometry. Model parameters will be calibrated against experimental data using inverse methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, physics-based modeling framework that moves beyond feature-specific correlations, enabling the prediction of heat transfer for novel surface designs. It will provide a publicly available dataset linking surface geometry to localized two-phase flow and heat transfer mechanisms, offering fundamental insight for the thermal design community.
proposals.novelty: The approach departs from adapting bulk correlations by deconstructing enhancement into additive, physics-based modules directly linked to surface feature geometry, a step towards a 'first-principles' design tool for enhanced surfaces. The concurrent use of modern diagnostics to quantify these discrete mechanisms for model calibration is a key advancement.

proposals.title: A Coupled Interfacial Drag and Heat Transfer Model for Mist Flow in Microfin Tubes: A Computational Framework for Compact Heat Exchanger Design
proposals.motivation: Mist flow, a high-quality annular flow regime, is critical for efficient heat transfer in compact evaporators and condensers, especially for high-pressure refrigerants like CO2. Current design tools treat interfacial drag (governing droplet dynamics) and convective heat transfer as decoupled phenomena, which is inadequate for enhanced-surface tubes where microfins drastically alter local film distribution and droplet interaction. This gap limits the performance optimization of next-generation compact heat exchangers for electronics cooling and high-flux thermal systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly coupling advanced interfacial drag correlations (for droplet deposition and entrainment) with a mechanistic wall heat transfer model will accurately predict the two-phase performance of microfin tubes in mist flow, revealing optimal fin geometries that maximize heat transfer by favorably modifying the local liquid film and droplet field.
proposals.method: The framework will integrate a 1D two-fluid model with source terms for interfacial momentum and energy exchange. We will adapt and implement pre-2026 interfacial shear and droplet flux correlations (e.g., from Okawa et al. or Fore & Dukler) into the solver. The heat transfer model will use a three-zone approach for the liquid film, vapor core, and droplets, with wall heat flux partitioning sensitive to local film thickness and droplet impact frequency, which are direct outputs of the coupled drag calculation. The system will be solved iteratively using a finite-volume method in MATLAB or Python, validated against existing experimental data for CO2 and R134a in microfin tubes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a unified computational tool for simulating mist flow in enhanced tubes, enabling performance prediction beyond empirical correlations. It will provide fundamental insights into the synergistic effects of surface geometry on interfacial dynamics and heat transfer, directly contributing to the design of high-performance, passive cooling systems for servers and high-heat-flux electronics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the two-way coupling within a single framework: the model dynamically links the interfacial shear and droplet behavior (from drag correlations) to the convective heat transfer coefficient, a step beyond applying independent, decoupled correlations for pressure drop and heat transfer.

proposals.title: A Generalized Heat Transfer and Pressure Drop Model for Flow Boiling of CO2 in Microfin and Enhanced-Surface Tubes
proposals.motivation: The use of CO2 (R744) as a low-GWP refrigerant is critical for sustainable cooling, particularly in high-heat-flux applications like data centers. Existing flow boiling models for CO2, including those developed by Thome's group, are primarily validated for smooth tubes, while commercially relevant systems increasingly employ microfin or other enhanced-surface tubes for superior thermal performance. A validated, generalized model for these surfaces is therefore essential for accurate system design and optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: The flow boiling heat transfer coefficient and frictional pressure drop for CO2 in enhanced-surface tubes can be accurately predicted by modifying existing mechanistic smooth-tube models with augmentation factors that are functions of the surface geometry (e.g., fin height, helix angle) and local flow conditions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the three-zone flow boiling model and separated flow pressure drop model from Thome's prior work. The core approach involves developing empirical augmentation factors (ε_h and ε_f) based on a new database of CO2 experiments in microfin tubes, compiled from literature and new validation experiments. These factors will be integrated into the smooth-tube model algorithms to modify the nucleate boiling contribution and the interfacial shear stress. The model will be implemented in an engineering tool and validated against independent data sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, physics-based design tool for predicting CO2 flow boiling performance in enhanced-surface tubes, directly addressing a gap in current engineering practice. It will enable more efficient and compact heat exchanger designs for electronics cooling and refrigeration systems, accelerating the adoption of CO2 technology.
proposals.novelty: While augmentation factors exist for other refrigerants, a validated model specifically for CO2, which has unique thermophysical properties, is lacking. The novelty lies in rigorously coupling the proven three-zone modeling framework with surface-specific parameters to create a generalizable prediction method for a next-generation refrigerant.

proposals.title: Generalized Validation of a Two-Phase Flow Boiling Model for Low-GWP Refrigerants and Mixtures in Passive Cooling Systems
proposals.motivation: The phase-out of high-GWP refrigerants under the Kigali Amendment and F-Gas regulations is driving rapid adoption of low-GWP alternatives like R1234ze(E), R1234yf, and natural fluids (e.g., CO2, propane). However, existing flow boiling heat transfer and pressure drop models, often calibrated for older fluids, show poor predictive accuracy for these new fluids and their mixtures, especially in passive systems like thermosiphons and pulsating heat pipes crucial for electronics and reactor cooling. This gap hinders the reliable design of next-generation, high-performance, passive thermal management systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A recently proposed mechanistic model for flow boiling and two-phase phenomena, developed based on data for CO2 and select fluids, can be generalized to accurately predict the thermal-hydraulic performance of a broad range of low-GWP pure refrigerants and azeotropic/zeotropic mixtures across macro- to micro-channel scales relevant to passive cooling devices.
proposals.method: We will compile an extensive, high-fidelity experimental database from literature for pure low-GWP refrigerants (R32, R1234ze(E), R1234yf, R290) and their mixtures, focusing on flow boiling heat transfer coefficient and pressure drop in channels from 0.5 mm to 5 mm. The core model, a separated flow model incorporating a modified Chen-type correlation for heat transfer and a drift-flux model for void fraction, will be implemented in a computational framework. Its empirical constants and closure relations (e.g., for nucleation suppression, convective enhancement) will be systematically recalibrated and validated using a k-fold cross-validation procedure, ensuring robustness against overfitting to specific fluid classes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, generalized predictive tool for two-phase performance of next-generation refrigerants, directly applicable to the design of thermosiphon and pulsating heat pipe cooling systems. It will identify key fluid properties and mixture effects governing performance in passive loops, offering practical guidelines for fluid selection and system optimization in electronics and industrial waste heat recovery.
proposals.novelty: While many studies validate models for single new fluids, this work systematically extends and recalibrates a unified mechanistic model for a diverse set of low-GWP pure fluids and their mixtures, explicitly targeting the operating regimes and channel sizes of passive cooling systems, which is a critical step beyond component-specific correlations.

proposals.title: A Coupled Interface-Resolved and System-Scale Model for Predicting Transient Dryout and Rewetting in Flow Boiling Systems
proposals.motivation: Predicting the Critical Heat Flux (CHF) and post-CHF behavior during transient events is a major challenge for the safe and efficient design of high-heat-flux cooling systems, such as those for electronics and energy reactors. Existing system-scale models rely on empirical correlations for dryout and rewetting, which lack a physical basis for dynamic conditions and fail to capture the feedback between local dry patch dynamics and global flow instabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly resolving the microlayer evaporation and dynamic contact line hysteresis at the three-phase interface will provide a physics-based closure for dry patch growth and recession, enabling accurate prediction of transient CHF and rewetting when coupled with a system-scale thermal-hydraulic model.
proposals.method: We will develop a high-fidelity, interface-tracking model using a Volume-of-Fluid (VOF) method within the OpenFOAM framework, enhanced with sub-grid models for microlayer evaporation and dynamic contact angle hysteresis. This local model will be two-way coupled with a system-scale code (e.g., a 1D thermal-hydraulic solver) using a domain decomposition approach, where the interface-resolved model acts as a local closure for the dryout front in critical regions like evaporator channels. The coupling will be managed via pre-2026 libraries like preCICE for partitioned multi-physics simulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, predictive simulation tool for transient two-phase flow boiling, moving beyond empirical CHF limits. It will provide unprecedented insight into the coupled local-global dynamics of dryout and rewetting, directly supporting the design of more robust passive cooling systems like advanced thermosiphons and pulsating heat pipes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct two-way coupling of an interface-resolved contact line model with a system-scale solver for practical geometries, explicitly linking microlayer physics to system-wide thermal-hydraulic oscillations during transients, a capability not present in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Network Solver with Enforced Global Balances for High-Fidelity Simulation of Maldistribution in Multi-Tube CO2 Evaporators
proposals.motivation: In high-heat-flux cooling systems, such as those for data centers or chemical reactors, multi-tube evaporators using CO2 are promising but prone to severe performance degradation due to flow maldistribution. Pre-2026 design tools often treat tubes as identical, replicating a single-tube model, which fails to capture the coupled shell-side flow distribution and tube-level two-phase dynamics that lead to instability and reduced heat transfer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly enforcing global mass and energy balances across a network of coupled, detailed tube models will accurately predict shell-side thermal gradients and flow distribution, enabling the simulation of maldistribution effects and system-level instability not captured by existing methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a coupled shell-and-tube network simulation framework. The shell-side will be solved as a flow network using a pressure-correction algorithm (e.g., a Newton-Raphson based solver) to compute the distribution of refrigerant to each tube. Each tube will be modeled with a high-resolution, one-dimensional flow boiling model for CO2, incorporating pre-2026 correlations for heat transfer and pressure drop. These tube models will be dynamically coupled to the network solver, with their inlet conditions updated iteratively until global balances are enforced across the entire evaporator.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, high-fidelity simulation tool for the design and optimization of compact, high-performance evaporators. It will provide new insights into the root causes of maldistribution in CO2 systems and enable the development of passive control strategies to mitigate instability, directly benefiting the design of next-generation cooling systems for electronics and industrial processes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, dynamic coupling of detailed, physics-based tube-level phase-change models within a system-level network solver that explicitly enforces global balances, moving beyond the common practice of simple kernel replication for multi-tube simulations.

proposals.title: A High-Fidelity, Orientation-Agnostic Simulation Framework for Shell-and-Tube Evaporators Using Dynamic Flow Pattern Mapping
proposals.motivation: The push for compact, high-performance thermal management in data centers and industrial processes demands non-conventional heat exchanger orientations, which introduce complex, orientation-dependent two-phase flow regimes. Existing system-level models often rely on simplified, single-regime correlations, failing to capture the maldistribution, instability, and performance degradation caused by the coexistence of different flow patterns (e.g., stratified, annular, slug) across a tube bundle. A predictive tool is needed to enable the design of reliable, orientation-agnostic evaporators for next-generation passive cooling systems and high-heat-flux equipment.
proposals.hypothesis: A full-scale shell-and-tube evaporator simulator that dynamically applies flow-pattern-dependent heat transfer and pressure drop correlations at the segment level will accurately predict system-level performance and instability, enabling the optimization of tube layout and orientation for compact, high-flux applications.
proposals.method: The approach builds a modular, tube-by-tube network model where each tube is discretized into short segments. A computationally efficient kernel will, for each segment and timestep, calculate local void fraction and flow parameters to dynamically identify the flow pattern (using a pre-2026 map such as Wojtan-Ursenbacher-Thome for horizontal flows and appropriate vertical/inclined maps). It will then select and apply the corresponding heat transfer (e.g., Thome et al. for CO2) and pressure drop correlations. The framework will be validated against experimental data from the researcher's prior work on CO2 evaporators and mini-scale pulsating heat pipes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open-source, full-scale simulation tool capable of predicting orientation-induced performance effects in shell-and-tube evaporators. It will provide a virtual testbed for designing compact heat exchangers for server cooling and industrial processes, directly linking tube-level flow physics to system-level reliability metrics like maldistribution and thermal runaway.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of dynamic, segment-level flow pattern mapping with a full-scale network model to simulate orientation-agnostic performance, a significant advancement over models using static, bundle-averaged correlations or single-orientation assumptions.

proposals.title: A Multi-Tube Kernel Approach for High-Fidelity Simulation of Shell-and-Tube CO2 Evaporator Performance
proposals.motivation: The design of efficient, compact shell-and-tube evaporators for CO2 refrigeration and heat pump systems is hindered by the complexity of two-phase flow distribution and heat transfer across multiple parallel tubes. Pre-2026 design tools often rely on overly simplified lumped-parameter or homogeneous models, which fail to capture maldistribution effects and localized dry-out that critically impact the performance and reliability of high-heat-flux cooling systems, such as those for data centers or industrial reactors.
proposals.hypothesis: A full-scale evaporator model, constructed by coupling parallel instances of a validated, high-fidelity single-tube flow boiling kernel, can accurately predict system-level performance degradation due to two-phase flow maldistribution and inter-tube thermal-hydraulic coupling under non-uniform shell-side heating.
proposals.method: The research will develop a modular simulation architecture where each tube is represented by a 1D discretized model kernel, based on Thome's prior work on CO2 flow boiling in micro-channels, incorporating local heat transfer coefficients and pressure drop correlations. These tube kernels will be coupled via a common shell-side fluid domain using a porous media approach to model the refrigerant distribution header and collection. The system will be solved iteratively using a pressure-correction algorithm (e.g., a SIMPLE-like method) to resolve the flow distribution. Validation will be performed against experimental data from a lab-scale, multi-tube CO2 evaporator test rig.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, physics-based modeling framework that bridges the gap between detailed tube-level phenomena and full-scale evaporator performance. It will provide designers with a tool to optimize tube layout, inlet conditions, and circuitry to mitigate maldistribution, directly contributing to the development of more efficient and reliable passive two-phase cooling systems for high-heat-flux applications.
proposals.novelty: While single-tube models exist, the explicit parallel coupling of multiple high-fidelity kernels to simulate shell-side driven maldistribution in a shell-and-tube geometry for CO2 is a novel computational approach, moving beyond averaged effectiveness-NTU methods to resolve localized thermal-hydraulic states.

proposals.title: A Mechanistic Heat Transfer Model for the Nucleation and Initial Growth Phase of Bubbles in Flow Boiling Systems
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of heat transfer during the initial bubble nucleation and growth phase is critical for designing high-performance, passive two-phase cooling systems, such as thermosiphons and pulsating heat pipes for electronics and reactors. Existing models often rely on empirical correlations that fail to capture the transient, high-heat-flux physics at the microscale, limiting the predictive design of next-generation cooling systems. A fundamental, mechanistic model is needed to bridge this gap and enable performance optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: The transient heat transfer during bubble nucleation and initial growth can be accurately modeled by coupling a microlayer evaporation model with a dynamic contact line analysis, where the dominant heat transfer mechanism shifts from transient conduction to microlayer evaporation within the first few milliseconds of bubble life.
proposals.method: The model will be developed by integrating a volume-of-fluid (VOF) framework in OpenFOAM with user-defined functions for nucleation physics. A sub-grid microlayer model, based on the work of Cooper & Lloyd (1969) and subsequent adaptations, will be implemented to account for evaporation in the thin liquid film beneath the growing bubble. The dynamic contact angle will be modeled using a hydrodynamic approach (e.g., Cox-Voinov relation). The model will be validated against high-speed, high-resolution experimental data for CO2 and refrigerants in micro-channels from the literature.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a predictive, physics-based component for system-level models of thermosiphons and pulsating heat pipes, improving the accuracy of startup and peak heat flux predictions. It will deliver a validated open-source simulation tool for researchers and engineers to analyze nucleation-dominated heat transfer in compact cooling systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted integration of a dynamic microlayer and contact line model specifically for the very initial growth phase (≤ 1 ms) within a practical CFD framework, moving beyond single-mechanism models or fully empirical correlations used in system design pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Coupled Thin-Film Evaporation and Pressure Drop Model for Predicting the Performance of Compact Thermosyphon and Micro-Channel Cooling Systems
proposals.motivation: The design of high-performance passive cooling systems, such as thermosyphons and micro-channel evaporators for electronics and reactors, requires accurate prediction of both heat transfer and hydraulic performance. Current models often treat heat transfer and pressure drop separately, leading to sub-optimal design and performance prediction, especially under high-heat-flux conditions where the two phenomena are intrinsically coupled. Integrating these models is critical for advancing the thermal management of next-generation servers and chemical reactors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that coupling a mechanistic thin-film evaporation heat transfer model with a concurrent, flow-regime-dependent two-phase pressure drop model will significantly improve the accuracy of predicting the overall thermal-hydraulic performance of compact cooling systems using refrigerants like CO2.
proposals.method: The research will extend an existing thin-film evaporation model (e.g., based on the meniscus profile and interfacial heat transfer) by integrating it with a separated flow pressure drop model that accounts for frictional, accelerational, and gravitational components. Specific algorithms will be developed to solve the coupled energy and momentum equations iteratively along the flow path in a channel, using flow regime maps (e.g., slug, annular) to select appropriate correlations for interfacial shear and void fraction. The model will be validated against experimental data from prior work on CO2 micro-channel evaporators and mini-scale pulsating heat pipes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified simulation tool for the co-design of heat transfer and fluid flow in passive cooling devices, enabling more efficient and reliable thermal management solutions. It will deliver new insights into the trade-offs between heat removal capability and pumping power (or natural circulation driving head) in systems operating near their performance limits.
proposals.novelty: While thin-film and pressure drop models exist independently, their tight, concurrent integration within a single, flow-regime-aware framework for design and analysis of compact cooling systems is novel. This moves beyond sequential or decoupled analysis common in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Molecular Dynamics Investigation of Near-Wall Evaporation Dynamics and Thermal Resistance for High-Performance Passive Cooling
proposals.motivation: The performance of passive two-phase cooling systems, such as thermosiphons and pulsating heat pipes, is critically limited by the evaporation process at the liquid-wall interface. Pre-2026 continuum models and correlations often fail to accurately predict the thermal resistance and heat flux limits in micro/nano-scale evaporators, especially for novel refrigerants like CO2, hindering the design of next-generation high-heat-flux cooling for electronics and reactors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that molecular dynamics simulations will reveal that the structure and dynamics of the first few liquid molecular layers near a solid wall, influenced by wall material and wettability, govern the evaporation flux and interfacial thermal resistance more significantly than bulk fluid properties.
proposals.method: We will employ classical molecular dynamics using LAMMPS with the OPLS-AA force field for organic refrigerants and a Lennard-Jones model for CO2. Solid walls will be modeled as fixed or semi-rigid FCC lattices (e.g., copper, silicon) with tunable wettability via solid-fluid interaction parameters. The simulation domain will consist of a liquid film in contact with a heated wall and a vapor region. The Velocity-Verlet algorithm will be used for integration, and we will compute evaporation rates, density/temperature profiles, and thermal resistance via direct heat flux calculation or the transient growth method.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide fundamental, atomistic insight into the microscopic mechanisms controlling near-wall evaporation, directly linking wall properties to evaporation kinetics. The results will yield quantitative data to inform and improve continuum-scale engineering models for evaporator design in high-performance passive cooling systems.
proposals.novelty: While MD has been used for bulk evaporation, its targeted application to model the solid-liquid-vapor contact line region with engineering-relevant wall materials and refrigerants, explicitly to decode the thermal resistance bottleneck in passive cooling devices, represents a novel and non-obvious integration of Thome's experimental expertise with high-fidelity simulation.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Microchannel Evaporators for a Target Heat Transfer Coefficient: A Computational Framework for CO2 Two-Phase Cooling Systems
proposals.motivation: The design of microchannel evaporators for high-heat-flux electronics cooling, particularly with low-GWP refrigerants like CO2, is currently an iterative, trial-and-error process. This is inefficient and often fails to achieve an optimal match between a component's thermal load and the cooling system's precise performance. An inverse design methodology, where geometry is derived from a target heat transfer coefficient (HTC), would enable first-pass optimal designs for specific server or reactor cooling applications, accelerating deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting validated forward models of CO2 flow boiling heat transfer and two-phase flow, a constrained optimization algorithm can reliably generate feasible microchannel geometries (hydraulic diameter, channel aspect ratio, length) that achieve a user-specified target HTC under given operating conditions.
proposals.method: The forward model will be built upon Dr. Thome's established flow boiling correlation and pressure drop model for CO2. This model will be embedded within an inverse optimization framework. A genetic algorithm or a gradient-based optimizer (e.g., Sequential Quadratic Programming) will be employed to iteratively adjust geometric parameters, minimizing the error between the model-predicted HTC and the target HTC. Constraints will include manufacturing limits, pressure drop ceilings, and flow stability criteria. Validation will be performed against existing experimental datasets from prior macro- and micro-channel studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational design tool that transitions microchannel evaporator design from a forward simulation task to an inverse specification task. It will provide clear guidelines on the trade-offs between geometry, pressure drop, and HTC for CO2 systems. The resulting framework can be directly applied to the design of next-generation passive thermosyphon and mini-scale PHP cooling systems for electronics and chemical reactors.
proposals.novelty: While extensive forward modeling of CO2 flow boiling exists, the explicit inversion of these models for direct geometric synthesis is a novel, non-obvious application. This approach moves beyond performance prediction to become an active design driver, which has not been systematically explored in the pre-2026 literature on CO2 microchannel cooling.

proposals.title: Experimental and Numerical Analysis of Flow Boiling Heat Transfer and Two-Phase Flow Patterns Under Non-Uniform Circumferential Heating in Micro-Channel Evaporators
proposals.motivation: In practical cooling applications like server racks or chemical reactors, heat flux is rarely uniform around a tube's circumference, leading to localized dryout, thermal stress, and suboptimal performance. Existing models and correlations for flow boiling, including those for CO2, predominantly assume uniform heating, creating a significant design-performance gap. Addressing this is critical for advancing the thermal management of high-heat-flux electronics and compact heat exchangers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that non-uniform circumferential heating significantly alters the local two-phase flow regime, critical heat flux (CHF), and heat transfer coefficient (HTC) compared to uniform heating predictions, and that these effects can be captured by modifying existing boiling correlations with a local heat flux parameter.
proposals.method: The research will employ an integrated experimental and computational approach. A test section with a transparent micro-channel tube, instrumented with thermocouples and pressure taps, will be subjected to controlled, asymmetric heating via segmented heaters. High-speed flow visualization will capture two-phase flow patterns. Data will inform the development and validation of a 3D Conjugate Heat Transfer (CHT) CFD model using the Volume of Fluid (VOF) method in ANSYS Fluent or OpenFOAM, incorporating user-defined functions (UDFs) for boiling models. The model will be used to systematically vary heating asymmetry and generate a modified correlation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset and a validated modeling framework for flow boiling under realistic non-uniform heating conditions in micro-channels. It will deliver an engineering correlation to predict local HTC and CHF degradation, enabling more accurate and reliable design of thermosyphons, pulsating heat pipes, and direct cooling systems for servers and reactors.
proposals.novelty: While non-uniform axial heating has been studied, the explicit focus on circumferential non-uniformity and its coupling with local flow regime evolution in micro-channels is novel. The integration of segmented experimental heating with 3D VOF-CFD to deconstruct the problem moves beyond the common uniform-heat-flux assumption in prior literature.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Heat Transfer Coefficient Model for Flow Boiling During Pattern Transitions in Micro/Mini-Channels
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of the heat transfer coefficient (HTC) in flow boiling is critical for designing efficient cooling systems for high-heat-flux electronics and compact heat exchangers. Existing steady-state HTC correlations often fail during dynamic operation, particularly during flow pattern transitions (e.g., slug to annular), leading to significant errors in thermal performance prediction and system reliability. A dynamic model is needed to capture the transient thermal-hydraulic coupling essential for advanced passive cooling systems like thermosiphons and pulsating heat pipes.
proposals.hypothesis: The local and instantaneous heat transfer coefficient during flow boiling can be robustly predicted by a physics-informed dynamic model that explicitly accounts for the evolving interfacial area and void fraction during flow pattern transitions. This model will outperform steady-state correlations under transient conditions.
proposals.method: The research will combine experimental characterization with dynamic modeling. Experiments will use high-speed flow visualization and synchronized local temperature/pressure measurements for CO2 and a dielectric fluid in a transparent micro/mini-channel evaporator to map HTC against identified flow patterns. A dynamic model will be developed by coupling a drift-flux model for void fraction evolution with a mechanistic, pattern-dependent HTC formulation. The model parameters will be optimized using a system identification approach (e.g., a Kalman filter) trained on the transient experimental data to capture the hysteresis and time constants of transition events.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, dynamic HTC model that can be integrated into system-level simulations of passive cooling devices, improving their design and control. It will provide new fundamental insight into the thermal inertia associated with flow pattern evolution, a key gap in pre-2026 two-phase flow knowledge.
proposals.novelty: While pattern-specific HTC correlations exist, a dynamic model that continuously adapts to the transitioning flow structure, using real-time estimable parameters like void fraction, represents a significant advance beyond the state-of-the-art static maps.

proposals.title: Thermal Performance and Flow Regime Mapping of a Multi-Evaporator, Multi-Condenser Pulsating Heat Pipe Network for Heterogeneous Multi-Chip Modules
proposals.motivation: Advanced computing systems, such as heterogeneous multi-chip modules (MCMs), integrate multiple high-heat-flux processors (CPUs, GPUs, accelerators) in a compact footprint, creating spatially and temporally variable thermal loads. Conventional single-evaporator, single-condenser pulsating heat pipes (PHPs) are ill-suited for this distributed, non-uniform heat dissipation, risking localized hotspots and suboptimal cooling performance. A network-based cooling architecture is required to manage the thermal interdependencies and ensure reliable operation of next-generation electronic packages.
proposals.hypothesis: A PHP network with multiple, distributed evaporators and condensers, interconnected via a shared fluidic manifold, will provide superior thermal homogenization and lower thermal resistance for an MCM under asymmetric heating compared to an array of independent, single-evaporator PHPs, by enabling dynamic, load-balancing fluid and vapor redistribution.
proposals.method: The research will employ an experimental approach using a transparent, modular PHP network fabricated from fluoropolymer (FEP) tubing with copper evaporator and condenser sections. The network will feature three discrete evaporator zones (simulating chips) and two condenser zones, connected in a closed loop with a shared adiabatic section. Flow visualization via high-speed imaging will be synchronized with thermal measurements (heat flux, temperature) to map two-phase flow regimes (slug, annular, chaotic) against thermal load distribution. Performance will be benchmarked against an equivalent array of three independent, single-evaporator PHPs under identical asymmetric power profiles, using overall thermal resistance and temperature uniformity as key metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic experimental dataset on the thermal-hydraulic behavior of multi-evaporator PHP networks under realistic MCM loading conditions. It will yield design guidelines for network topology (e.g., series vs. parallel evaporator connections) and charge ratio optimization. The results will advance passive two-phase cooling from single-component to multi-component system-level thermal management.
proposals.novelty: While multi-evaporator loop heat pipes and thermosyphons exist, applying the distributed network concept to the inherently chaotic, self-sustained oscillatory flow of a PHP is unexplored. The investigation focuses on the emergent system-level behavior—how flow interactions between evaporators can enhance or degrade performance—rather than the performance of isolated PHP units.

proposals.title: Experimental and Analytical Investigation of Pulsating Heat Pipe Performance Under Spatially Non-Uniform Heat Flux Conditions Representative of Modern Processors
proposals.motivation: Modern high-performance computing and server CPUs generate highly non-uniform heat flux distributions, with hotspots far exceeding the package average. Existing PHP research and design guidelines predominantly assume uniform heating, which fails to capture the complex local thermo-hydraulic phenomena and can lead to suboptimal or unreliable cooling solutions. This gap limits the effective application of passive, high-performance PHP cooling to next-generation electronic equipment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a PHP's thermal resistance, operational stability, and dry-out limit are significantly degraded under realistic, non-uniform heat loads compared to uniform heating of equal total power, due to localized flow instabilities and premature evaporator dry-out in high-flux zones.
proposals.method: A closed-loop PHP with a transparent evaporator section will be instrumented with temperature sensors and a high-speed camera. A custom multi-zone heater block, capable of independent power control to 5-7 discrete zones, will replicate documented CPU heat flux maps (e.g., from Intel or AMD architectures circa 2020-2025). Experiments will systematically vary hotspot intensity, location, and total power for a working fluid like R134a or HFO-1234yf. Performance metrics (thermal resistance, temperature uniformity, operational limits) will be compared against uniform heating baselines. Data will inform a semi-empirical model correlating local flux disparity with overall PHP performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated dataset and performance correlations for PHPs under engineered non-uniform heating, moving beyond idealized laboratory conditions. It will yield practical design guidelines for integrating PHPs with real processor layouts, identifying critical hotspot thresholds and optimal fluid filling ratios for uneven loads. The findings will directly advance the development of robust, passive cooling systems for high-heat-flux servers.
proposals.novelty: While non-uniform heating is acknowledged as a real-world condition, its systematic, parametric experimental study in PHPs—using heater arrays replicating actual commercial processor flux patterns—is novel. This approach shifts the design paradigm from treating the PHP as a uniformly loaded component to a spatially responsive system.
