proposals.title: Enhancement of Pulsating Heat Pipe Performance for High-Heat-Flux Electronics: An Integrated Study of Non-Uniform Channel Geometry and Hybrid Wick Structures
proposals.motivation: The thermal management of high-performance computing and power electronics is increasingly constrained by heat fluxes exceeding 25 W/cm². Passive cooling solutions like Pulsating Heat Pipes (PHPs) are attractive for their simplicity and efficiency, but their performance plateaus at these high fluxes due to flow instabilities and dryout phenomena. Advancing PHP design is critical to support next-generation electronic systems without resorting to complex, energy-intensive active cooling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategically combining a non-uniform channel cross-section (converging in the evaporator, diverging in the condenser) with a localized hybrid wick structure in the evaporator will synergistically enhance thin-film evaporation and liquid replenishment, thereby suppressing dryout and enabling stable operation at heat fluxes significantly above 25 W/cm².
proposals.method: The study will employ a parametric experimental approach using a modular, transparent PHP test apparatus with water as the working fluid. We will fabricate and test PHPs with varying evaporator channel geometries (e.g., tapered, stepped reductions) and integrate selective wick structures (e.g., sintered copper powder or micro-grooves) only in the evaporator section. Performance will be evaluated by measuring thermal resistance, temperature oscillation amplitude, and the critical heat flux at a 70% fill ratio. High-speed flow visualization will be used to correlate two-phase flow patterns (slug/plug, annular) with thermal performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated design framework for high-flux PHPs, quantifying the individual and combined effects of channel geometry modification and selective wicking. It will deliver new empirical data and flow regime maps linking specific geometric parameters to dryout limits, offering practical guidelines for thermal engineers designing passive cooling for servers and power electronics.
proposals.novelty: While non-uniform channels and wicks have been studied separately, their integrated application in a PHP is novel. The proposed design specifically targets the evaporator's fluid dynamics and phase-change mechanisms, a focused approach distinct from global wicking or uniform channel optimization prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Experimental Screening of Low-GWP Refrigerants for Pulsating Heat Pipe Performance in High-Heat-Flux Electronics Cooling
proposals.motivation: The impending phase-down of high-GWP hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs) under the Kigali Amendment creates an urgent need for sustainable, high-performance cooling fluids. Pulsating Heat Pipes (PHPs) are promising for electronics thermal management, but their performance is highly sensitive to fluid thermophysical properties, and systematic data on next-generation, zero/low-GWP refrigerants (e.g., HFOs, natural fluids, and their blends) is scarce for these dynamic two-phase systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific low-GWP refrigerants, selected based on a comprehensive property screening, will outperform conventional fluids like R134a in PHP thermal resistance and critical heat flux, due to optimized combinations of latent heat, viscosity, and surface tension that enhance pulsation stability.
proposals.method: We will construct a closed-loop, multi-turn, copper capillary PHP test apparatus with a transparent polycarbonate visual section. A controlled, uniform heat load will be applied to the evaporator (simulating a server chip), with condenser temperature precisely regulated. We will systematically test a matrix of pre-2026 candidate fluids: R1234ze(E), R1234yf, R600a, R290, CO2, and a commercial HFO/HFC blend (e.g., R513A). Performance will be benchmarked against R134a. Key metrics—thermal resistance, start-up behavior, temperature/pressure oscillation amplitude, and dry-out limit—will be measured for varying fill ratios (40-70%) and heat fluxes (up to 30 W/cm²).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-of-its-kind comparative performance database linking low-GWP fluid properties to PHP operational characteristics. It will provide actionable guidelines for selecting environmentally benign working fluids for high-heat-flux passive cooling systems, directly informing the design of next-generation, sustainable server and electronics cooling solutions.
proposals.novelty: While PHP studies with single low-GWP fluids exist, a systematic, head-to-head experimental comparison of a broad range of zero/low-GWP candidates—including natural refrigerants and blends under identical PHP geometry and boundary conditions—is novel and addresses a critical knowledge gap for sustainable thermal design.

proposals.title: Co-Designed Evaporator and Advanced Thermal Interface for a Pulsating Heat Pipe: Minimizing System Thermal Resistance for High-Heat-Flux Electronics
proposals.motivation: Pulsating heat pipes (PHPs) are promising passive cooling solutions for high-power electronics, but their system-level performance is bottlenecked by the thermal interface resistance between the heat source and the PHP evaporator. Recent studies, including Thome's work, report a significant system resistance of ~0.065 K/W, largely attributed to this interface. Reducing this resistance is critical for meeting the escalating thermal demands of next-generation servers and computing hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the co-design of the PHP evaporator's contact surface geometry with the microstructure and compliance of next-generation thermal interface materials (TIMs), such as graphene-enhanced composites or phase-change metal alloys, will synergistically reduce the total thermal joint resistance, enabling a system resistance below 0.04 K/W.
proposals.method: The research will employ a coupled experimental and analytical approach. First, a parametric study will fabricate custom PHP evaporator sections with varied micro-channel and land area patterns. These will be tested against advanced TIMs (e.g., Indium-based sheets, graphene-filled greases) using a dedicated thermal interface resistance tester. A 3D conjugate heat transfer model, validated with infrared thermography and high-speed flow visualization of the PHP, will be developed to optimize the spatial heat flux distribution from the TIM into the PHP's working fluid. The design goal is to match the TIM's effective conductivity and thickness with the PHP's local boiling initiation requirements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated co-design framework for integrating PHPs with advanced TIMs, quantifying the trade-offs between material properties and evaporator geometry. It will deliver a prototype demonstrating a sub-0.04 K/W system thermal resistance, a benchmark for passive two-phase cooling systems. The results will be presented as practical guidelines for thermal engineers designing next-generation server cooling modules.
proposals.novelty: While PHPs and TIMs are studied separately, their co-design is novel, moving beyond simple material substitution to a systems-level optimization of the thermal joint. The approach explicitly links the TIM's thermophysical properties to the PHP's internal two-phase flow dynamics to minimize the total resistance, a non-obvious integration not detailed in prior literature.

proposals.title: Internal Diagnostics of a Pulsating Heat Pipe Using an Integrated Microfabricated Sensor Network for High-Resolution Spatiotemporal Two-Phase Flow Mapping
proposals.motivation: Pulsating Heat Pipes (PHPs) are promising passive cooling solutions for high-heat-flux electronics, but their complex, chaotic internal two-phase flow dynamics are not fully understood, limiting predictive modeling and performance optimization. Current diagnostics rely on external measurements, which lack the spatial and temporal resolution to capture critical local phenomena like dryout, nucleation, and flow regime transitions within the capillary channels. A method for direct, distributed internal sensing is needed to unlock the fundamental thermo-fluidic mechanisms governing PHP operation.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating an array of microfabricated temperature and pressure sensors directly onto the internal wall of a PHP capillary will enable high-fidelity, real-time mapping of the two-phase flow, revealing direct correlations between local thermal-hydraulic conditions and overall thermal performance.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a transparent, silicon-based PHP with rectangular microchannels. Using MEMS techniques, thin-film platinum resistance temperature detectors (RTDs) and piezoresistive pressure sensors will be patterned and passivated onto the internal channel walls at strategic axial and azimuthal locations. The sensor network will be connected via integrated traces to a multiplexed data acquisition system. The instrumented PHP will be tested under varying heat loads, fill ratios, and orientations. Sensor data will be synchronized with high-speed flow visualization and external thermal performance measurements to correlate internal events with global heat transfer coefficients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, spatially-resolved dataset of temperature and pressure fluctuations inside an operating PHP, creating an unprecedented benchmark for validating and refining two-phase flow and heat transfer models. It will establish a novel diagnostic platform for passive two-phase devices and deliver concrete design guidelines for enhancing PHP reliability and thermal performance in electronics cooling applications.
proposals.novelty: While MEMS sensors have been used in macro-scale two-phase flow studies, their direct integration into the internal, confined geometry of a PHP for distributed, wall-mounted sensing is novel. This approach moves beyond external observation to provide direct, quantitative internal state measurements previously inaccessible.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Framework for Predicting the Thermal Performance of Passive Two-Phase Cooling Systems
proposals.motivation: The design of passive cooling systems like thermosyphons and pulsating heat pipes for electronics and reactors relies heavily on empirical correlations, which struggle with the complex, non-linear interactions between fluid properties, fill ratio, heat load, and geometry. This leads to suboptimal designs and extensive prototyping cycles. A predictive model based on experimental data can accelerate the development of efficient, high-heat-flux cooling solutions critical for data centers and energy systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models, trained on a comprehensive experimental dataset, can predict the thermal resistance of passive two-phase cooling systems with greater accuracy than traditional semi-empirical correlations by capturing complex, non-linear parameter interactions.
proposals.method: We will compile a structured dataset from our own experiments (on CO2, water, and refrigerants) and published literature, featuring inputs like fluid properties (e.g., reduced pressure, latent heat), fill ratio, heat load, and inclination. We will train and compare several regression models, including Gradient Boosting Machines (e.g., XGBoost) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), using k-fold cross-validation. Hyperparameter optimization will be performed via Bayesian methods to maximize prediction accuracy on the thermal resistance target variable.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, data-driven predictive tool for thermal designers, reducing reliance on iterative prototyping. It will also identify and rank the most influential parameters on system performance, providing new physical insights into the operational limits of passive two-phase cooling devices.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to active cooling systems, its targeted use for performance prediction in purely passive, gravity-assisted, and pulsating two-phase systems—integrating diverse working fluids and scales from micro-channels to macro-scale reactors—represents a significant, unexplored advancement beyond current correlation-based approaches.

proposals.title: Experimental and Numerical Investigation of a Large-Scale Pulsating Heat Pipe Array for Passive Waste Heat Recovery from Data Centers to Building Heating Systems
proposals.motivation: Data centers are major energy consumers, with a significant portion of input power dissipated as low-grade waste heat. Recovering this heat for building space heating can dramatically improve overall energy efficiency and reduce operational carbon footprints. However, conventional active heat recovery systems add complexity and parasitic energy use, creating a need for robust, passive, and scalable solutions.
proposals.hypothesis: A modular, large-scale pulsating heat pipe (PHP) array can be engineered to passively and reliably transfer waste heat from server rack exhaust streams to a building's hydronic heating circuit, achieving a target thermal conductance and heat recovery efficiency that justifies the capital investment.
proposals.method: The research will employ a combined experimental and numerical approach. First, a modular PHP panel will be designed, featuring multiple parallel stainless steel tubes (ID 3-5 mm) charged with a low-GWP refrigerant (e.g., R1234ze(E)). An experimental test rig will integrate a simulated server rack heat source and a building-side hydronic heat sink. Performance will be characterized under varying heat loads (1-10 kW), sink temperatures, and inclinations. Concurrently, a 1D lumped parameter model, incorporating pre-2026 flow regime maps and heat transfer correlations, will be developed in MATLAB/Simulink to simulate the complex two-phase thermo-fluid dynamics and guide scale-up.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated design methodology and performance database for scaling PHPs from electronic cooling to building-scale energy recovery. It will provide concrete guidelines on optimal geometry, working fluid, and operating conditions for this novel application, advancing the practical implementation of passive heat recovery in the built environment.
proposals.novelty: While PHPs are well-studied for micro-electronics, their application as a primary heat exchanger for building-scale waste heat recovery is unexplored. The novelty lies in addressing the unique challenges of scaling, modular integration, and reliable operation under the variable thermal conditions of a data center and a building heating loop.

proposals.title: A Self-Sensing Pulsating Heat Pipe: Correlating Flow Oscillation Signatures to Thermal Performance and Degradation for Dual-Function Cooling and Health Monitoring
proposals.motivation: Pulsating Heat Pipes (PHPs) are promising passive cooling solutions for high-heat-flux electronics and energy systems, but their performance is sensitive to internal conditions like partial dry-out or non-condensable gas generation. Current health monitoring requires external sensors, adding complexity and failure points. A method to infer thermal state and degradation directly from the PHP's intrinsic oscillatory behavior would enable predictive maintenance and robust system design.
proposals.hypothesis: The spectral and temporal characteristics of the self-sustained flow oscillations in a PHP are uniquely correlated with its instantaneous thermal performance (effective thermal resistance) and internal health state (e.g., charge ratio degradation, non-condensable gas presence), enabling the PHP to function as its own sensor.
proposals.method: We will instrument a closed-loop PHP, similar to Thome's mini-scale designs, with high-frequency pressure transducers at the evaporator and condenser and local temperature sensors. The PHP will be tested under controlled heat loads and fill ratios, including introduced non-condensable gas to simulate degradation. Time-series analysis (Fast Fourier Transform, wavelet analysis) will extract features (dominant frequency, amplitude, signal entropy) from the pressure oscillation data. Machine learning regression models (e.g., Random Forest, Support Vector Regression) will be trained to map these flow signatures to simultaneously measured thermal resistance and known degradation states.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational correlation database between PHP flow dynamics and system health, moving beyond mere thermal characterization. It will deliver a novel, non-invasive diagnostic framework that transforms a PHP from a passive component into an active sensing element, contributing directly to the reliability of passively cooled server racks and compact thermal management systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes PHP oscillations for performance optimization, the explicit dual-use of the PHP's inherent flow instability as a built-in diagnostic sensor for its own thermal state and degradation is a novel paradigm shift in two-phase passive thermal management.

proposals.title: Fabrication and Thermal Performance of Ultra-Thin, High-Density Channel Flexible Pulsating Heat Pipes for Wearable High-Power Electronics
proposals.motivation: The advancement of wearable and conformal electronics, such as augmented reality glasses, medical monitoring patches, and flexible displays, is constrained by thermal management. These devices generate localized high heat fluxes but require cooling solutions that are thin, lightweight, flexible, and passive to maintain user comfort and device reliability. Existing rigid heat sinks or active fans are unsuitable, creating a critical gap in thermal management technology for next-generation wearable systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by scaling the channel density and optimizing the channel cross-section within a thin, flexible polymer substrate, a flexible pulsating heat pipe (F-PHP) can be engineered to achieve a significant reduction in thermal resistance and an increase in critical heat flux, enabling effective cooling of high-power wearable devices under dynamic bending conditions.
proposals.method: The research will employ a systematic design-of-experiments approach. First, F-PHPs will be fabricated using laser-ablated polyimide films to create channel arrays with varying densities (5-20 channels/cm) and hydraulic diameters (0.3-0.8 mm). CO2 will be selected as the working fluid for its favorable flow boiling characteristics in micro-channels. The experimental setup will involve a custom flexible heater and infrared thermography to map temperature distribution and measure thermal resistance under controlled heat loads (5-50 W/cm²) and bending radii (flat to 20 mm). Performance will be benchmarked against a baseline low-density F-PHP and a solid copper film of equivalent thickness.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative relationship between channel density, flexibility, and thermal performance in F-PHPs, providing design guidelines for integrating high-efficiency passive cooling into wearable systems. It will deliver a prototype demonstrating a >30% reduction in peak temperature compared to existing flexible conductive spreaders at target heat fluxes, directly addressing a key bottleneck in wearable technology development.
proposals.novelty: While flexible heat pipes exist, this work uniquely focuses on scaling the internal channel density within the severe geometric constraints of a wearable form factor to enhance two-phase flow and heat transfer, a parameter space not systematically explored in pre-2026 literature on flexible PHPs.

proposals.title: Active Suppression of Nucleate Boiling Onset in Forced Convection Cooling Systems for High-Power Electronics
proposals.motivation: In high-heat-flux electronics cooling, the onset of nucleate boiling (ONB) introduces thermal resistance hysteresis, flow instabilities, and potential dryout, limiting system reliability and controllability. Current designs aim to manage boiling after it occurs. A paradigm shift to actively delay ONB would enable stable, single-phase operation at higher heat fluxes, crucial for next-generation servers and power electronics where thermal transients are severe.
proposals.hypothesis: By strategically tailoring surface energy, micro-structure, and local fluid properties at the channel wall, the wall superheat required for nucleate boiling inception can be significantly increased under forced convection conditions, extending the stable single-phase heat transfer regime.
proposals.method: The research will employ an integrated experimental and analytical approach. A closed-loop test facility with a high-precision micro-channel evaporator will be constructed, using a dielectric fluid (e.g., HFE-7100) as the working fluid. Surface engineering methods, including deposition of ultra-thin, low-surface-energy coatings (e.g., fluoropolymer) and laser-etching of specific sub-micron cavity patterns, will be applied to the heated surfaces. Experiments will systematically vary heat flux, mass flux, and subcooling while monitoring local temperature and pressure to detect ONB. Data will be analyzed against classical nucleation theory and force-balance models modified for flow conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational design methodology for 'boiling-suppressive' surfaces in forced convection. It will provide quantitative criteria (e.g., an extended ONB map) and surface design rules to enhance the stability and predictability of high-performance cooling systems, directly benefiting thermal management of data centers and power electronics.
proposals.novelty: While extensive pre-2026 research focuses on enhancing boiling heat transfer, this proposal inverts the problem to deliberately suppress nucleation. It moves beyond passive surface modification for boiling enhancement to active, design-led control of the phase change initiation point under flow conditions.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Transient Heat Flux in Two-Phase Cooling Systems Using Wall Temperature Measurements
proposals.motivation: Accurate knowledge of the spatial and temporal heat flux distribution is critical for the design and health monitoring of high-heat-flux cooling systems, such as server racks and chemical reactors using thermosyphons or pulsating heat pipes. Direct measurement of heat flux is often impractical or intrusive. An inverse method to deduce heat flux from easily measured wall temperatures would enable performance optimization and fault detection in these systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That a physics-informed inverse algorithm, constrained by the governing equations of two-phase flow and heat transfer, can accurately reconstruct the applied heat flux field from transient wall temperature profiles, even in the presence of measurement noise and complex phase-change phenomena.
proposals.method: The method will combine a forward model (a 1D/2D conjugate heat transfer model coupled with a two-phase flow regime map, validated against the researcher's prior CO2 and thermosyphon data) with an inverse solver. The inverse problem will be formulated as an optimization task minimizing the difference between measured and simulated wall temperatures. Specific algorithms to be tested include the Conjugate Gradient Method with Adjoint Equations and Tikhonov regularization to handle ill-posedness. The approach will be validated using experimental data from instrumented mini-scale pulsating heat pipe and macro-channel CO2 evaporator setups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, non-intrusive diagnostic tool for thermal management systems, allowing engineers to map heat flux in real-time. It will advance the field of inverse heat transfer by specifically addressing the challenges of two-phase flows with moving boundaries and regime transitions, contributing to safer and more efficient cooling system design.
proposals.novelty: While inverse heat conduction problems are established for solids, applying them to dynamically coupled solid-fluid systems with boiling and condensation, and using the solution to inform two-phase flow performance metrics, represents a significant and non-trivial extension of the method.

proposals.title: Integration of a Verified Low-Cost Two-Phase Flow Model into Open-Source Heat Exchanger Design Software for Enhanced Passive Cooling System Simulation
proposals.motivation: The design of efficient passive cooling systems, such as thermosiphons and pulsating heat pipes, relies on accurate modeling of two-phase flow and heat transfer. However, existing commercial heat exchanger design software often lacks robust, computationally efficient models for these complex phenomena, particularly for novel refrigerants like CO2, limiting their utility for advanced thermal management in electronics and chemical reactors. Integrating a verified, low-calculation-effort model into accessible design tools would democratize high-fidelity simulation for engineers.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a previously verified, simplified two-phase flow model—derived from experimental data on CO2 and other working fluids—into an open-source heat exchanger design framework will enable accurate, rapid simulation of passive cooling systems without the prohibitive computational cost of high-fidelity CFD.
proposals.method: The project will implement the researcher's existing low-order model, validated for CO2 and other fluids in macro/micro-channels, as a new module within the open-source software HTRI Xchanger Suite® or a comparable open-source platform like COMSOL's LiveLink™ or a custom Python-based framework. The integration will involve developing a dedicated API or plugin architecture, mapping the model's inputs (heat flux, fluid properties, geometry) to the software's internal data structures. The model's algorithms for flow regime transition, heat transfer coefficient, and pressure drop will be encoded, and validation will be performed against the researcher's existing experimental datasets for thermosiphon and PHP systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the thermal engineering community with a practical, open-access tool for designing high-performance passive cooling systems, bridging the gap between specialized academic models and industrial design workflows. It will demonstrate a pathway for translating fundamental two-phase flow research into applied software, potentially improving the design efficiency of cooling for servers, power electronics, and chemical reactors.
proposals.novelty: While two-phase models exist, their direct integration into general-purpose, open-source heat exchanger design software as a dedicated, computationally efficient module for passive cooling components is not commonplace. This moves beyond publishing model coefficients to creating a directly usable engineering tool.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale Modeling Framework for Shell-and-Tube Condenser Performance Prediction Using Validated Local Heat Transfer Coefficients
proposals.motivation: Shell-and-tube condensers are critical for industrial waste heat recovery and large-scale cooling systems, but their design relies on empirical correlations with limited accuracy for novel, high-performance refrigerants like CO2. Scaling up validated local heat transfer models from single micro/macro-channels to complex multi-tube bundles with shell-side flow maldistribution remains a significant challenge, hindering the optimization of next-generation, compact, and passive thermal management systems for data centers and chemical reactors.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical modeling framework, integrating a validated local condensation coefficient model for individual tubes with a flow distribution network solver, can accurately predict the overall thermal-hydraulic performance of a multi-tube shell-and-tube condenser under varying operating conditions.
proposals.method: The research will employ a three-step computational and experimental approach. First, a local heat transfer and pressure drop model for in-tube condensation, previously validated for CO2 in Thome's work, will serve as the foundational element. Second, this model will be integrated into a 1D network solver that accounts for the shell-side flow distribution across tube rows, using a porous media approach or a nodal resistance network based on pre-2026 shell-side correlations. Third, the framework will be validated against experimental data from a purpose-built, instrumented lab-scale shell-and-tube condenser using a refrigerant like R134a or CO2, measuring overall heat duty, pressure drops, and localized tube wall temperatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a predictive, physics-based design tool for shell-and-tube condensers that moves beyond lumped-parameter approaches, enabling more efficient and compact heat exchanger designs. It will directly contribute to improving the performance of passive cooling systems, such as advanced thermosyphons for server racks or industrial reactors, by providing a validated scaling methodology from single tubes to full bundles.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit multi-scale coupling of a high-fidelity local phase-change model with a shell-side flow distribution solver to predict overall condenser performance, a synthesis not commonly found in pre-2026 literature which often treats bundle effects separately or empirically.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Neural Network Framework for Predicting Two-Phase Flow Regimes and Heat Transfer in Cross-Flow Tube Bundles
proposals.motivation: The design of high-performance heat exchangers, such as those for power generation, chemical processing, and advanced electronics cooling, relies on accurate predictions of two-phase flow and heat transfer in tube bundles. Current methods, based on empirical correlations or computationally expensive CFD, struggle with the complex, coupled phenomena of flow regime transitions, void fraction, and local heat transfer coefficients across diverse bundle geometries and operating conditions. A robust predictive tool is needed to accelerate the design of novel, compact heat exchangers without exhaustive testing for each new configuration.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid framework that integrates mechanistic physics models with a machine learning surrogate, explicitly constrained by conservation laws and flow regime criteria, can accurately predict local and bundle-averaged heat transfer and pressure drop for boiling and condensation in cross-flow tube bundles, enabling reliable extrapolation beyond its training data.
proposals.method: The framework will be built in three stages. First, a high-fidelity dataset will be generated using validated Volume-of-Fluid (VOF) CFD simulations and local mechanistic models (e.g., for nucleate boiling and film condensation) for a parameterized range of tube pitches, diameters, and flow conditions. Second, a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) will be developed, where the loss function incorporates the Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes equations, energy conservation, and closure relations for interfacial forces. Third, a separate classifier network, trained on flow regime maps from the dataset, will provide a physics-based prior to inform the PINN's predictions of regime-dependent heat transfer mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, computationally efficient surrogate model for two-phase tube bundle performance, validated against existing experimental data. It will provide new insights into the coupling between bundle geometry, emergent flow patterns, and thermal performance. The resulting open-source framework will serve as a practical design tool for thermal engineers, reducing reliance on costly prototype testing.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a flow regime classifier as a physics-based constraint within a PINN architecture for tube bundle flows is a novel approach. This moves beyond purely data-driven or correlation-based methods by embedding mechanistic knowledge of regime transitions directly into the machine learning model's structure, enhancing its predictive robustness and physical consistency.

proposals.title: Experimental and Modeling Framework for High-Accuracy Two-Phase Thermophysical Properties of Low-GWP Refrigerants and Petrochemicals
proposals.motivation: The transition to low-global-warming-potential (GWP) refrigerants and complex petrochemical mixtures is critical for sustainable cooling and chemical processes. However, existing property databases lack sufficient high-fidelity experimental data, especially under the dynamic two-phase conditions prevalent in modern heat exchangers and thermosyphons. This data gap leads to significant uncertainty in the design and performance prediction of energy-efficient systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A targeted experimental campaign, focused on boiling heat transfer coefficients and pressure drop for next-generation fluids (e.g., R1234ze(E), R1234yf, and selected hydrocarbon blends) under realistic flow regimes, will reveal systematic deviations from current predictive models. Integrating this data into a machine-learning-augmented property framework will yield more accurate and generalizable correlations.
proposals.method: The research will employ a closed-loop, instrumented thermosyphon test rig, capable of operating as a macro/micro-channel evaporator, to collect new two-phase flow boiling data for selected fluids. Measurements will include local heat transfer coefficients, pressure gradients, and flow pattern visualization. This experimental dataset will be used to train and validate a hybrid property model, combining a fundamental equation of state (e.g., GERG-2008 or REFPROP structure) with a Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) algorithm to correct for systematic biases in existing heat transfer and pressure drop correlations specific to two-phase flow.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, high-accuracy dataset for critical two-phase properties of modern working fluids. It will deliver an open-source, modular property calculation module that improves the predictive fidelity for system-level simulations of passive cooling devices and compact heat exchangers, directly impacting design efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While property models exist, this approach uniquely integrates targeted thermosyphon-based experimentation—a direct proxy for passive cooling applications—with a non-parametric machine learning correction specifically tuned for the two-phase regime, moving beyond pure fluid equilibrium properties to system-relevant transport phenomena.

proposals.title: Experimental Validation of Two-Phase Flow and Heat Transfer Correlations for CO2 and Water at Extreme Pressures and Temperatures in Advanced Heat Exchanger Geometries
proposals.motivation: Industrial applications like high-temperature chemical reactors, next-generation concentrated solar power, and high-heat-flux electronics cooling are pushing heat exchangers into extreme operating regimes (e.g., near-critical pressures, cryogenic temperatures, or supercritical CO2 cycles). Pre-2026 design relies heavily on empirical correlations validated under moderate conditions, creating significant uncertainty and over-design in these frontier applications, which impacts system efficiency, cost, and reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: Existing widely-used correlations for flow boiling heat transfer coefficient and two-phase pressure drop (e.g., Chen, Kandlikar, Müller-Steinhagen & Heck) will exhibit significant predictive deviations (>±30%) when applied to CO2 and water at extreme pressures (from sub-critical to supercritical for CO2; up to 20 MPa for water) and temperatures, particularly in enhanced micro/macro-channel geometries relevant to compact heat exchangers.
proposals.method: The research will employ a closed-loop, instrumented experimental test facility capable of precise pressure (0.1-30 MPa) and temperature (-40°C to 300°C) control. A dedicated test section will feature interchangeable evaporator plates with micro-channel and macro-channel geometries. Systematic experiments will measure local heat transfer coefficients and pressure drops for CO2 and water across the targeted extreme conditions. Data will be statistically compared against predictions from a suite of pre-2026 correlations using root-mean-square error (RMSE) and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) analyses to quantify deviations and identify failure regimes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a high-fidelity, extreme-condition dataset for two key working fluids, directly informing the design of next-generation thermal systems. It will yield clear, quantitative guidance on the validity ranges of existing models and identify specific physical phenomena (e.g., property variation effects, flow regime transitions) that cause correlation breakdown, laying groundwork for future model development.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies validate correlations for individual fluids or geometries, this work systematically targets the combined extreme of pressure, temperature, *and* geometry for both CO2 (a low-global-warming-potential refrigerant) and water, directly testing model limits in the simultaneous parameter space most relevant to cutting-edge industrial challenges pre-2026.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Two-Phase Cooling Systems: A Multi-Objective Optimization Framework for Specified Heat Duty in Thermosyphons and Pulsating Heat Pipes
proposals.motivation: Current two-phase cooling system design is iterative and forward-driven, where a geometry is specified and its performance is evaluated. For high-heat-flux applications like data center servers and chemical reactors, engineers need to rapidly design systems meeting precise thermal demands. An inverse methodology, starting from the required heat duty, would significantly accelerate and optimize the design of passive cooling loops.
proposals.hypothesis: A constrained multi-objective optimization framework, integrating validated two-phase flow and heat transfer correlations, can inversely solve for a Pareto-optimal set of pipe geometries (diameter, length) and operating conditions (filling ratio, pressure) that satisfy a target heat duty while minimizing pressure drop and system volume.
proposals.method: The method will develop an inverse design tool by coupling a forward two-phase flow model (using established correlations for flow boiling and pressure drop, e.g., for CO2 and other refrigerants) with a genetic algorithm for optimization. The forward model, validated against Thome's prior experimental data, will serve as the fitness evaluator. The optimization algorithm will treat geometry and flow parameters as variables, with the target heat duty and practical constraints (e.g., max allowable pressure, orientation) as fixed inputs, iterating to find optimal design sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, automated design methodology for two-phase passive cooling systems, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will yield a practical software tool and design charts linking target heat duties to optimal configurations, directly applicable to electronics cooling and process industry reactor design.
proposals.novelty: While forward simulation of two-phase systems is well-established, applying multi-objective optimization to solve the inverse design problem for a specified heat duty—particularly for passive systems like thermosyphons and pulsating heat pipes—is a novel and non-trivial integration of thermal engineering and computational optimization.

proposals.title: Experimental and Analytical Investigation of Concentration-Driven Phase Change in Binary Fluid Thermosyphons for Passive Cooling
proposals.motivation: Passive cooling systems like thermosyphons and pulsating heat pipes rely on phase change driven by temperature gradients. However, in applications involving non-azeotropic mixtures (e.g., CO2 blends, absorption fluids), phase change can be significantly driven by concentration gradients, a coupled heat and mass transfer problem poorly characterized in compact, passive devices. Understanding this dual-driven mechanism is crucial for designing next-generation, high-performance cooling for electronics and reactors where fluid composition varies or is actively managed.
proposals.hypothesis: In a closed-loop thermosyphon charged with a zeotropic binary mixture, the local boiling and condensation rates, and thus the overall thermal resistance, will be governed by the coupled interfacial temperature and concentration gradients, deviating significantly from pure-fluid or azeotropic predictions.
proposals.method: We will construct an instrumented, visual thermosyphon test rig charged with a binary mixture (e.g., CO2/propane). High-speed imaging and synchronized measurements of local temperature and pressure will track two-phase flow regimes. The experimental data will be used to develop and validate a 1-D transient model coupling the energy equation with a species conservation equation. The model will incorporate existing pre-2026 mass transfer correlations (e.g., the film model) and be solved using a finite-volume method to predict local concentration fields and their impact on the phase change heat transfer coefficient.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed dataset and validated modeling framework for concentration-driven phase change in a passive thermosyphon geometry. It will yield new design guidelines for using zeotropic mixtures to enhance thermal performance or maintain stability in high-heat-flux cooling. The results will extend the fundamental understanding of two-phase flow with mass diffusion beyond large-scale chemical process equipment to compact thermal management systems.
proposals.novelty: While concentration-driven phase change is studied in distillation, its role in dictating the thermal-hydraulic performance of a closed-loop, gravity-driven passive cooling device is unexplored. This bridges Thome's expertise in compact two-phase cooling with the fundamental mass transfer problem, moving beyond the standard assumption of phase equilibrium controlled solely by temperature and pressure.

proposals.title: A Network-Level Modeling Framework for the Dynamic Performance of Multi-Stream Phase-Change Heat Exchangers
proposals.motivation: The design of high-performance cooling systems for data centers and chemical reactors increasingly relies on networks of compact, phase-change heat exchangers like thermosyphons and micro-channel evaporators. Current design tools, however, are largely based on single-tube or single-stream correlations, neglecting the complex thermal and hydraulic interactions between multiple boiling and condensing streams in a network. This gap limits the ability to predict system-level stability, transient response, and optimal configuration, leading to over-designed or under-performing thermal management systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dynamic performance and stability limits of a multi-stream phase-change heat exchanger network are governed by non-linear interactions between local two-phase flow regimes and global pressure-temperature coupling, which can be captured by a coupled 1D/0D modeling framework.
proposals.method: The research will develop a hierarchical modeling architecture. First, validated 1D models for individual components (e.g., CO2 micro-channel evaporators, thermosyphon condensers) will be implemented using pre-2026 flow boiling and condensation correlations. These component models will then be integrated into a network solver using a pressure-correction algorithm (e.g., a modified SIMPLE method) to handle the coupled fluid flow and heat transfer. The system's dynamic response will be simulated by solving the transient energy and mass conservation equations for each node, with specific attention to flow maldistribution and instability thresholds during start-up and load changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-of-its-kind, open-source simulation tool for designing and analyzing networks of phase-change heat exchangers. It will provide new insights into system-level two-phase flow instabilities and thermal coupling, moving beyond single-component analysis. The results will directly inform the engineering of more efficient and reliable passive cooling systems for high-heat-flux electronics and industrial reactors.
proposals.novelty: While extensive literature exists on single two-phase channels, the explicit modeling of dynamic, two-way interactions between multiple, interconnected boiling and condensing streams within a complete network represents a significant and non-trivial scale-up in complexity. The proposed framework uniquely bridges detailed component physics with system-level performance prediction.

ideas.title: Full-Scale, Orientation-Agnostic Shell-and-Tube Evaporator Simulator with Flow Pattern Mapping
ideas.description: This research synthesizes tube-level modeling with orientation-dependent flow physics to develop a high-fidelity, full-scale shell-and-tube evaporator simulator. The core innovation is a computationally efficient kernel that dynamically applies flow-pattern-dependent heat transfer and pressure drop correlations based on local conditions within each tube segment, whether horizontal, vertical, or inclined. This enables the simulation to predict system-level performance degradation, maldistribution, and instability caused by the coexistence of different flow regimes and void fractions across the tube bundle. The resulting tool provides a virtual testbed for optimizing tube layout, orientation, and operating conditions for next-generation, compact, and non-conventionally oriented heat exchangers.

ideas.title: Coupled interfacial drag and heat transfer modeling for mist flow in enhanced-surface tubes
ideas.description: This research direction integrates advanced interfacial drag correlations from annular flow with a detailed heat transfer model for tubes featuring microfins or other enhanced surfaces. The core synergy is modeling the two-way coupling: the enhanced surface geometry alters the local droplet deposition, re-entrainment, and interfacial shear, which in turn modifies the convective heat transfer coefficient and the wall-to-droplet thermal interaction. This creates a unified computational framework that moves beyond treating drag and heat transfer as independent phenomena, allowing for the design of next-generation enhanced tubes specifically tailored for high-quality mist flow applications in compact heat exchangers.

ideas.title: Coupled Surface-Heat Flux Effects on Flow Pattern Dynamics
ideas.description: This research investigates the coupled, non-linear interaction between tube surface characteristics (engineered roughness and wettability) and the magnitude of applied heat flux in dictating two-phase flow pattern transitions. It posits that heat flux does not merely shift pre-existing boundaries but fundamentally alters the effective surface properties experienced by the flow, such as dynamic contact angle and nucleation site density, which in turn feedback to modify the heat transfer mechanism and flow regime. The synergy enables the development of a new class of predictive maps or models where transition criteria are functions of both the static surface morphology and the dynamic, flux-dependent thermal-hydraulic conditions at the wall. This moves beyond examining independent factors to understanding their inseparable role in governing system stability and performance.

ideas.title: Mechanistic modeling and experimental mapping of enhancement physics for generalized tube surface design
ideas.description: This research develops a robust, generalized model for heat transfer in enhanced surface tubes by moving beyond correlation adaptation to a physics-based framework. The core innovation is a modular model that explicitly represents the fundamental flow and heat transfer mechanisms (e.g., vortex generation, thin-film renewal) induced by specific surface features. This theoretical work is coupled with a focused experimental program using modern flow and thermal diagnostics to measure these mechanisms directly, providing calibration data and validating the model's predictive capability across a defined design space of geometries, fluids, and flow regimes.

ideas.title: Coupled Flow Pattern and Heat Transfer Dynamics in Inclined Tubes with Circumferential Wall Temperature Variation
ideas.description: This research investigates the interdependent relationship between non-uniform circumferential heating and two-phase flow patterns in inclined and vertical tubes. It develops advanced models and experimental maps that account for how localized heating or cooling around the tube wall induces circumferential temperature gradients, which in turn generate buoyancy-driven secondary flows. These secondary flows significantly alter the phase distribution, transition boundaries between flow regimes (e.g., slug to annular), and overall heat transfer coefficients compared to either uniform-wall or horizontal-tube assumptions. The core novelty is capturing this two-way coupling where the wall thermal boundary condition dictates the flow pattern, and the evolving flow pattern subsequently dictates the local heat transfer around the circumference.

ideas.title: Predictive Modeling of Forced Convection Response to Circumferentially Non-Uniform Thermal Boundary Conditions
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond descriptive studies to develop a predictive framework for internal flows subjected to engineered, non-uniform heat flux. It combines detailed experimental data from a tunable, segmented-heater test section with companion RANS and LES simulations to isolate the governing flow physics. The core outcome is a validated, reduced-order model that correlates imposed circumferential heat flux variations with resultant flow field modifications and system-level thermal performance, providing design guidelines for applications like advanced nuclear fuel bundles, electronics cooling, and high-performance heat exchangers.

ideas.title: Coupled Shell-and-Tube Network Simulation with Enforced Global Balances
ideas.description: This approach develops a high-fidelity simulation for multi-tube evaporators by integrating detailed tube-level phase-change models into a system-level network solver. The framework explicitly calculates shell-side flow distribution and thermal gradients, dynamically coupling them to the conditions at each tube's inlet. This ensures mass and energy balances are maintained across all tubes, accurately predicting maldistribution effects, system instability, and overall performance. The result is a robust tool for design and optimization that overcomes the limitations of simple kernel replication.

ideas.title: Active Microfluidic Control of Two-Phase Flow Regimes to Delay Dryout Onset
ideas.description: This research investigates the active control of flow pattern transitions in microchannel horizontal tubes to deliberately delay the onset of dryout. By integrating real-time sensing of local flow regimes (e.g., annular to mist flow transitions) with responsive micro-actuation (e.g., localized heating, surface energy modification, or pressure pulsations), the system treats the dryout condition as a control target rather than a mere prediction. This enables the dynamic stabilization of desirable, heat-transfer-efficient flow patterns (like stable annular flow) and the active suppression of regimes that precipitate dryout, leading to novel control strategies for ultra-compact thermal management systems.

ideas.title: A Physics-Informed Hybrid Framework for Predicting Two-Phase Flow and Heat Transfer in Tube Bundles
ideas.description: This research develops a predictive framework for boiling and condensation in tube bundles by integrating mechanistic physics models, targeted high-fidelity simulations, and machine learning. The core uses validated CFD and local physics models to generate a high-quality, physically consistent dataset covering key flow regimes and geometric parameters. A machine learning surrogate model, constrained by fundamental conservation laws and flow regime transitions, is then trained on this dataset. This hybrid approach provides robust, extrapolative predictions for heat transfer and pressure drop across novel bundle designs, significantly reducing reliance on exhaustive experimental testing for each new configuration.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Functional Dependence of Flow Regime Transitions on Spatiotemporally Varying Heat Flux
ideas.description: This research systematically investigates how the magnitude and spatial/temporal distribution of an applied heat flux modify the transition criteria between convective flow regimes (e.g., from laminar to turbulent, or between different pattern states). The approach employs a combined experimental and numerical framework using surfaces with programmable, patterned heat flux to decouple the effects of total power from its distribution. The outcome will be a generalized scaling model that predicts transition boundaries as a function of key dimensionless groups incorporating flux non-uniformity, moving beyond the assumption of a uniform or singular heat flux condition.

ideas.title: Investigate the dynamic co-evolution of flow patterns and local heat flux in systems with imposed non-uniform heating
ideas.description: This research direction challenges the foundational assumption that flow pattern maps are independent of the heating condition by studying them under actively non-uniform heat flux. It investigates how the spatial variation of heat flux not only imposes a new boundary condition but also dynamically interacts with the flow regime transitions it induces. The core novelty is examining the feedback loop: local transitions (e.g., from bubbly to slug flow) alter heat transfer coefficients, which modifies the effective local heat flux and thermal field, thereby shifting adjacent transition boundaries. This creates a coupled, co-evolutionary system where the flow pattern map and the heat flux distribution are interdependent and must be solved for simultaneously.

ideas.title: Interface-Resolved Dynamic Model for Transient Dryout Front Propagation and Rewetting
ideas.description: This research develops a high-fidelity, interface-tracking model to simulate the dynamic propagation of dryout fronts and the rewetting process during flow boiling transients. The model explicitly resolves the microlayer evaporation and dynamic contact angle hysteresis at the three-phase contact line, providing physics-based closure for dry patch growth and recession. It is coupled with a system-scale thermal-hydraulic code to capture the feedback between local dryout dynamics and global flow oscillations, enabling prediction of critical heat flux (CHF) and post-CHF behavior under realistic transient scenarios.

ideas.title: Machine Learning-Driven Inverse Modeling of Circumferential Heat Flux from Flow Regime Data
ideas.description: This research fuses non-uniform circumferential wall temperature modeling with machine learning-based flow pattern recognition to develop an inverse heat transfer solution. A machine learning model is trained on high-fidelity simulation or experimental data to recognize not just the flow regime, but also the characteristic thermal and hydrodynamic responses induced by specific, non-uniform heating patterns around the tube. The trained system can then analyze real-time sensor data (e.g., temperature, pressure, void fraction) to simultaneously classify the flow regime and infer the most probable circumferential wall temperature distribution causing the observed state. This enables predictive control of heating systems to achieve desired thermal performance or avoid critical heat flux conditions, moving beyond passive classification to active, model-informed thermal management.

ideas.title: Machine Learning-Enabled Dynamic Dryout Prediction via Real-Time Flow Regime Recognition
ideas.description: This research fuses a predictive dryout model with machine learning-based flow pattern recognition to create a dynamic, condition-aware framework for condensation systems. The core innovation is using real-time sensor data (e.g., pressure, temperature, void fraction) to classify the instantaneous flow regime, which then serves as a critical input to a physics-informed machine learning model that predicts the imminent risk of film dryout. This synergy allows the dryout prediction to adapt to changing system dynamics and inlet conditions, moving beyond static correlations. The resulting capability enables proactive system control to prevent dryout, optimizing heat transfer performance and reliability in applications like power plant condensers and aerospace thermal management.

ideas.title: Constrained Multi-Objective Optimization for Tube Design Targeting HTC Profiles
ideas.description: This research develops a computational framework to find physically realizable tube designs that approximate a desired heat transfer coefficient (HTC) profile. Instead of a direct inverse solution, the method treats it as a constrained optimization problem, minimizing the error between the target and achieved HTC while enforcing critical secondary requirements like allowable pressure drop, minimal wall thickness, and geometric smoothness. The output is a set of Pareto-optimal design candidates, providing engineers with a range of viable solutions that balance thermal performance with other practical constraints. This approach ensures the resulting designs are not only thermally targeted but also feasible for real-world application.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Potential Field Analysis for Circumferential Wall Temperature Modeling
ideas.description: This idea challenges the common engineering assumption of a uniform circumferential wall temperature in tube flow heat transfer. By drawing an analogy from geophysics, where potential fields from non-uniform subsurface sources are analyzed, we adapt the solution method of harmonic decomposition. The circumferential wall temperature distribution is represented as a Fourier series (analogous to spherical harmonics). This harmonic boundary condition is then used in the energy equation to solve for the resulting three-dimensional temperature field in the fluid, moving from a 1D or 2D averaged model to a 3D conjugate model that captures local hot/cold spots and their effect on overall heat transfer performance and thermal stresses.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Compartment Models for Transient Flow Boiling and Dryout Dynamics
ideas.description: This research adapts the compartmental modeling approach from epidemiology to develop a dynamic version of a flow boiling and dryout model. In epidemiology, populations are divided into states (Susceptible, Infected, Recovered), and differential equations govern the flow between them based on dynamic rates. Analogously, the fluid and wall in a heated channel are divided into compartments representing distinct thermodynamic and wetting states (e.g., subcooled liquid, flowing two-phase mixture, superheated vapor, wetted wall, dry wall). A system of coupled ordinary differential equations is developed where the 'flow' of mass and energy between these compartments is governed by dynamic, state-dependent rates for processes like nucleation, bubble departure, dry patch formation, and re-wetting. These rates are functions of the transient heating and flow conditions. This low-order dynamic model can simulate the temporal evolution of the system, predicting the onset of dryout, its propagation, and potential recovery, capturing key nonlinearities and thresholds inherent in the transient boiling crisis, much like compartment models capture epidemic dynamics.

ideas.title: Adapting Metastability Control from Materials Science to Delay Dryout Onset
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of metastability control from materials science to the problem of dryout onset in thermal-hydraulic systems. In materials science, the onset of an undesirable phase transition (e.g., nucleation of a brittle phase) is treated not as a fixed prediction but as a kinetic barrier to be actively raised through manipulation of thermodynamic and kinetic parameters—such as adding grain refiners, applying external fields, or controlling cooling rates—to extend the metastable state. Adapted to dryout, the thermodynamic state preceding dryout (thin film flow, nucleate boiling) is conceptualized as a metastable state. The research idea is to design active control systems that manipulate local flow parameters—such as modulating pressure transients, dynamically distributing wall heat flux, altering surface wettability in real-time, or injecting minute nucleation agents—to increase the energy barrier for the nucleation and growth of a dry patch. This fundamentally shifts the engineering objective from forecasting an inevitable failure point to dynamically sustaining the safe, wetted operational state, thereby inverting dryout onset from a prediction target to a controllable parameter.

ideas.title: Physics-based dimensionless framework for generalized two-phase flow pattern prediction
ideas.description: This research develops a generalized flow pattern prediction framework by first identifying the critical dimensionless groups (e.g., Reynolds, Jakob, Bond, Convection numbers) that govern transitions for diverse fluid classes, including water, ammonia, and nanofluids. A comprehensive experimental database, spanning these fluids and a wide range of operating conditions, is compiled to map the observed flow patterns within this dimensionless space. The core outcome is a physics-based, validated correlation or machine learning model that predicts flow regime transitions as functions of the identified dimensionless parameters, explicitly accounting for property variations. This approach moves beyond direct map extension to create a robust, scalable predictive tool for non-refrigerant working fluids.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Tube Geometry via Acoustic Impedance Matching Methods
ideas.description: This idea adapts inverse solution methods from acoustics, specifically the design of multi-layer structures to match a target acoustic impedance profile, to the thermal domain. The analogous problem is designing an acoustic absorber or filter for a desired frequency-dependent impedance curve, which is solved by segmenting the device and using iterative optimization to tune segment properties. Adapted to the original research seed, the tube is treated as a series of discrete axial segments. An inverse design algorithm systematically varies the local geometry (e.g., diameter, internal surface features) and possibly inlet conditions for each segment. By minimizing the error between the simulated and target Heat Transfer Coefficient (HTC) profile along the tube length, the required tube geometry and operational parameters are determined, effectively solving the thermal inverse problem.

ideas.title: Pressure-Pattern-Informed Control for Active Dryout Onset Delay
ideas.description: This research fuses the concepts of active dryout delay and flow-pattern inference from pressure signals into a novel control paradigm for two-phase flow systems. The core idea is to use high-frequency pressure drop measurements as the sole input to a real-time classifier that identifies the instantaneous flow pattern (e.g., annular, slug, churn). This identified pattern, rather than a predicted dryout point, becomes the direct control target. The controller then calculates and implements adjustments (e.g., to mass flux, heat flux, or inlet subcooling) specifically designed to maintain the system in a flow pattern furthest from the dryout-onset transition, thereby actively delaying dryout through pattern stabilization.

ideas.title: Adapting Tomographic Reconstruction to Map Flow Response to Circumferential Heat Flux Variations
ideas.description: This idea challenges the uniform heat flux assumption by drawing an analogy from medical imaging and non-destructive testing. In computed tomography, a 3D internal structure is reconstructed from multiple 1D or 2D projection measurements taken at different angles. The analogous solution uses inverse algorithms to solve for the internal map from indirect data. Adapted to this thermal-fluid context, the research would involve designing an experiment where a circumferentially varying heat flux is applied to a flow (e.g., in a pipe or over a surface). An array of sensors around the circumference collects integrated response data (temperature, heat transfer coefficient, flow deflection) for different programmed heat flux 'projection' patterns. Using adapted tomographic reconstruction algorithms, the complete 3D flow and thermal field—showing how the flow redistributes itself in response to the non-uniform heating—can be mathematically reconstructed, providing a foundational map for validating advanced simulations and improving system design.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmaceutical Blending Process Maps for Zeotropic Refrigerant Flow Patterns
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that pure refrigerant flow pattern criteria apply to mixtures by proposing the development of specific criteria for zeotropic or azeotropic refrigerant mixtures. Drawing an analogy from pharmaceutical manufacturing, where process maps successfully predict the complex behavior of powder blends, the solution is adapted. In pharmaceuticals, regime maps using dimensionless groups define boundaries between mixing, segregation, and granulation outcomes. Adapted to refrigeration, the research would develop analogous flow pattern regime maps for refrigerant mixtures. This involves identifying and validating key governing dimensionless parameters—incorporating mixture-specific properties like temperature glide and composition-dependent transport properties—to create predictive diagrams that map the transitions between flow patterns (e.g., annular, stratified, slug) in heat exchanger tubes. These maps would enable more accurate design and optimization of systems using next-generation mixture refrigerants.

ideas.title: Validate thermodynamic model using a cross-domain benchmark database
ideas.description: This research adapts a validation strategy from computational chemistry to the field of refrigerant thermodynamics. In drug discovery, models are tested against large, diverse benchmark databases (e.g., PDBbind) to assess generalizability. The analogous solution is to create a curated, cross-domain benchmark database for fluid properties, containing high-quality experimental data for a broad spectrum of pure and mixed refrigerants, including next-generation alternatives. The adapted idea is to rigorously validate the proposed thermodynamic model against this comprehensive database, testing its performance across different chemical families, mixture compositions, and state conditions. This systematic benchmarking will identify the model's strengths, limitations, and failure modes, providing a robust assessment of its predictive power and guiding future development to improve accuracy for novel fluid systems.

ideas.title: Physics-Based, Orientation-Adaptive Flow Pattern Modeling Framework for Vertical and Inclined Geometries
ideas.description: This research develops a next-generation, physics-based modeling framework for gas-liquid flows in vertical and inclined tubes that fundamentally accounts for the shifting force balance caused by orientation. It begins with a comprehensive experimental campaign to establish new flow pattern transition criteria and void fraction correlations specifically for non-horizontal orientations, capturing the dominant role of buoyancy. The core model dynamically adjusts its closure relations for interfacial friction, pressure drop, and heat transfer using a dimensionless scaling parameter that weights the contribution of gravitational forces. This results in a unified, predictive tool that robustly transitions between horizontal, inclined, and vertical flow regimes based on first principles rather than geometric analogy.

ideas.title: Predicting Condensation Dryout Onset via Ecological Tipping Point Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodology for predicting ecological regime shifts to the problem of liquid film dryout onset during condensation. In ecology, the approach to a tipping point (e.g., a lake shifting to a eutrophic state) is signaled by statistical early-warning indicators like increased autocorrelation and variance in system state data. Analogously, for a condensing film, we propose to treat local film thickness measurements as the system's state variable. The adapted idea is to develop a predictive model that analyzes real-time or simulated high-resolution data of the film's dynamics. By monitoring for precursors like increasing spatial correlation of thin-film regions and rising variance in local thickness, the model would identify the loss of hydrodynamic stability and forecast the dryout onset probabilistically, providing an early-warning diagnostic for heat exchanger performance and control.

ideas.title: Proactive Maintenance of a Dryout-Resilient Thermal-Hydraulic State
ideas.description: This research inverts the control paradigm from reacting to dryout onset to proactively sustaining a system state far from its occurrence. It defines a quantifiable 'dryout resilience margin' derived from real-time estimates of local thermal-hydraulic conditions. A predictive controller then continuously adjusts system parameters (like flow rate, pressure, or heat flux distribution) to maximize this margin. This approach ensures control actions are physically feasible and prevents the system from entering the regime where rapid, uncontrollable dryout initiation is imminent.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Surface-State-Aware Predictive Framework for Condensation Film Dryout Onset
ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic predictive framework for condensation dryout onset that explicitly accounts for the temporal evolution of surface state. It integrates in-situ, spatially resolved diagnostics (e.g., micro-thermocouple arrays, optical interferometry) with an adaptive machine learning model trained on both initial surface characteristics and real-time sensor data. The system continuously updates its dryout predictions based on measured changes in local wettability, micro-scale film dynamics, and heat flux, moving beyond static correlations. This results in a robust, self-correcting model applicable to real-world condensing surfaces subject to aging, fouling, and operational transients.

ideas.title: Physics-Based Generalized Flow Regime Transition Model for Arbitrary Tube Inclination
ideas.description: This research moves beyond creating discrete maps for specific inclinations by developing a unified, predictive model for flow regime transitions in tubes at any orientation. The core approach is to identify the critical dimensionless groups (e.g., modified Froude, Weber, and Reynolds numbers) that govern the competition between inertial, viscous, surface tension, and body force components as the direction of gravity changes relative to the flow axis. Using a combination of carefully controlled experiments across a wide parameter space and direct numerical simulation, the project will deconstruct the effect of inclination into its fundamental physical drivers. The outcome will be a generalized transition criterion or a compact, physics-informed machine learning model that can accurately predict flow patterns for any inclination, fluid properties, and flow rates, significantly enhancing predictive capability for engineering design.

ideas.title: Lattice-Boltzmann Kernel for Full-Scale Evaporator Simulation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by using analogical reasoning from porous media CFD. The analogous problem of simulating macroscopic flow through a complex porous structure, solved using a Representative Elementary Volume (REV) as a repeatable computational kernel, is adapted. Here, the detailed 'tube-level model' acts as the REV kernel. The adapted research idea is to formalize this kernel and embed it within a lattice framework representing the full tube arrangement. By solving the kernel locally and coupling kernels through inter-cell communication for shell-side conditions, the method scales the detailed tube physics to a full multi-tube evaporator simulation, balancing accuracy with computational feasibility, much like lattice methods scale pore-level physics to reservoir-scale models.

ideas.title: Validate condensation flow pattern map using geophysical fluid regime mapping techniques
ideas.description: This research adapts validation techniques from geophysical fluid dynamics, specifically the methods used to map and verify large-scale ocean flow regimes, to the problem of validating a flow pattern map for condensation inside horizontal tubes. In oceanography, theoretical regime maps are tested by combining scaled laboratory experiments with field data from diverse observation systems, using dimensionless parameters and statistical pattern recognition to correlate observations with predictions. The adapted idea applies this multi-scale, parameter-space approach to condensation flows: first, establish a relevant dimensionless parameter space (e.g., a modified Lockhart-Martinelli parameter for condensing flows); second, design a systematic experimental campaign that varies these parameters across different tube scales and working fluids; third, employ both local high-resolution diagnostics (like PIV or laser-induced fluorescence) and global flow visualization to capture pattern transitions; and finally, use statistical analysis to map the observed condensation patterns (annular, wavy, slug, bubbly) onto the proposed theoretical map, rigorously testing its predictive accuracy and identifying the conditions for regime transitions.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Transfer Learning for Generalized Flow Pattern Recognition Across Refrigerant Classes
ideas.description: This research develops a machine learning framework that integrates physical constraints and domain knowledge into a neural network trained on flow visualization data for a subset of refrigerants. The model's core is designed to extract latent features representing fundamental thermo-physical drivers of flow patterns, rather than just memorizing fluid-specific visual signatures. This physics-informed feature space is then leveraged via transfer learning, allowing the model to be rapidly and accurately validated and adapted to a broad database of alternative refrigerants and mixtures with minimal new data. The synergy yields a generalizable predictive tool that elucidates the universal physics of multiphase flow while solving the practical problem of pattern classification for new, data-scarce working fluids.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Calibration of Interfacial Drag in Mist Flow via High-Fidelity PIV Measurements
ideas.description: This research direction integrates Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) with advanced interfacial drag modeling to fundamentally advance mist flow prediction. High-fidelity PIV measurements of both droplet and gas phase velocity fields provide unprecedented local data on slip velocities and interfacial momentum transfer. This experimental data is used not for simple validation, but for the direct, spatially-resolved calibration and development of next-generation interfacial drag correlations adapted from annular flow physics. The synergy creates a data-driven, physics-based methodology to replace current empirical mist flow models with mechanistically sound closures, dramatically improving the predictive accuracy of CFD simulations for applications in aerospace propulsion, nuclear reactor safety, and chemical process engineering.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Succession Models to Map Heat-Flux-Dependent Flow Regime Transitions
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that flow regime transition boundaries are independent of the applied thermal forcing. Drawing an analogy from ecology—where ecosystem state transitions (e.g., forest to savanna) depend critically on the nature of external disturbances like fire frequency—we adapt the framework of ecological state-and-transition models (STMs). The analogous solution involves modeling transitions as functions of the disturbance regime, not just static environmental gradients. Adapted to thermal-fluid systems, the research idea is to construct dynamic flow regime maps where the transition boundaries between patterns (e.g., bubbly, slug, churn flow) are explicitly modeled as surfaces dependent on the magnitude, rate of change, and spatial distribution of the applied heat flux. This treats the heat flux not merely as a parameter moving the system along a fixed map, but as a control variable that actively modifies the map's topology, stability basins, and transition probabilities.

ideas.title: Generalized Flow-Pattern-Dependent Model for Large-Diameter Pipes in Arbitrary Orientations
ideas.description: This research develops a comprehensive, validated flow-pattern-dependent model applicable to large-diameter pipes (e.g., >0.1 m) across the full range of orientations from vertical to horizontal. It systematically investigates the interaction between pipe diameter, inclination angle, and flow pattern transitions, which is critical for industrial systems where pipe runs are not purely horizontal. The outcome will be a generalized correlation or mechanistic model that accounts for scale and orientation effects simultaneously, providing a essential tool for the design and operational analysis of complex piping in power plants and other large-scale industrial facilities.

ideas.title: Active Dryout Delay via Real-Time PIV-Based Flow Control
ideas.description: This research integrates Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) for high-resolution, local velocity field mapping in two-phase flows with a control-theoretic approach that inverts dryout onset as a target. The PIV system provides real-time, spatially detailed measurements of liquid film thickness and interfacial velocity, which serve as the feedback signal for a model-predictive controller. The controller calculates optimal adjustments to system parameters (e.g., inlet pulsation, wall heating profile, or flow obstructions) to actively manipulate the local flow field, thickening the liquid film or enhancing replenishment precisely where dryout is incipient. Thus, dryout is not merely predicted but actively delayed through direct, data-driven intervention in the local hydrodynamic conditions.
