ideas.description: This approach systematically identifies the most challenging regimes and refrigerant mixtures for which the proposed model is likely to fail, based on its theoretical foundations and training data boundaries. It then designs a validation campaign that prioritizes acquiring or generating high-quality benchmark data specifically for these identified adversarial cases. The model is tested against this targeted, gap-filling data to rigorously assess its extrapolation capability and fundamental physical accuracy. This moves validation from a passive check on available data to an active stress-test of the model's predictive limits and robustness.

ideas.title: Topological Early-Warning System for Condensation Dryout Onset
ideas.description: This research fuses topological data analysis (TDA) with condensation dryout prediction by applying persistent homology to time-series or spatially resolved images of the condensing liquid film. The method quantifies the evolution of topological features—such as the birth and death of holes representing dry patches or the connectivity of the liquid network—as the system approaches dryout. This provides a direct, data-driven metric for the structural instability of the film, correlating specific topological signatures with the imminent onset of dryout. The resulting model offers a novel early-warning capability based on pattern shape evolution, complementing and potentially surpassing conventional models reliant on local thickness or heat flux measurements.

ideas.title: Adapt aerosol deposition models from atmospheric science for mist flow interfacial drag
ideas.description: This idea proposes a method transfer from atmospheric science to multiphase flow modeling. The core analogy is between the mist flow regime (dispersed liquid droplets in a continuous gas core in pipes) and the transport of aerosol particles (e.g., fog droplets) in the atmospheric boundary layer. The analogous solution from atmospheric science involves using advanced microphysical and deposition models that account for polydisperse droplet size distributions, turbulence-droplet interactions, and deposition mechanisms to accurately predict momentum and mass transfer. This solution is adapted by formalizing the interfacial drag in mist flow not as a simple bulk property, but by incorporating aerosol physics-based correlations for droplet drag, turbulent diffusion, and deposition velocities. This enables a more fundamental, predictive model for interfacial shear stress in mist flow, improving accuracy in scenarios with high entrainment fractions and complex droplet size distributions, directly parallel to how atmospheric models predict aerosol behavior.

ideas.title: Multi-fidelity scaling framework for flow mapping in large-diameter industrial pipes
ideas.description: This research develops a systematic framework to overcome the non-linear scaling challenges when applying sensor-based flow maps from laboratory to industrial pipe diameters. It integrates high-fidelity computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations to predict scaling discontinuities in turbulence and installation effects, guiding the design of a modular large-scale test facility. The experimental rig enables direct measurement and correction of the flow map under industrially-relevant diameter-to-sensor ratios and flow regimes. The final output is a validated scaling protocol and corrected mapping methodology, robustly applicable to power plant piping systems.

ideas.title: Apply Darcy-Brinkman-Forchheimer modeling to stratified-wavy film dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Darcy-Brinkman-Forchheimer (DBF) modeling framework from geophysical fluid dynamics (specifically, flow in deformable porous media like aquifers) to the problem of stratified-wavy liquid films in multiphase flow. The analogous solution uses the DBF equations to unify the description of flow in heterogeneous, deformable subsurface formations by combining Darcy flow, interfacial shear (Brinkman), and inertial drag (Forchheimer). Adapted to the research context, the wavy liquid film is conceptualized as a dynamic, anisotropic porous medium. The adapted model uses a variable porosity field tied to the local film thickness and wave geometry. The DBF framework is then applied, where the Darcy term represents the base film flow resistance, the Brinkman term resolves shear stress transmission from the gas phase at the interface, and the Forchheimer term accounts for form drag induced by large wave structures. Coupled with a closure for medium 'deformation' (i.e., the relationship between local pressure and film height), this provides a continuum-based method to predict momentum transport and film dynamics without explicitly resolving every wave, analogous to porous media treatments of complex subsurface flows.

ideas.title: Constrained Multi-Objective Inverse Design of Stable, Realizable Tube Flows
ideas.description: This research develops a computational framework to solve the ill-posed inverse design problem for internal flows by finding tube geometries and inlet conditions that produce a target flow pattern. The system reformulates the problem as a constrained multi-objective optimization, simultaneously targeting pattern fidelity, hydrodynamic stability, and energy efficiency. It employs an adjoint method for efficient gradient calculation and explores the Pareto-optimal trade-off surface between these competing objectives. The final output is a set of manufacturable, physically realizable designs that are robust to small perturbations in operating conditions.

ideas.title: Quantifying Coupled Roughness-Wettability Synergy on Flow Pattern Transitions
ideas.description: This research systematically investigates the non-linear interaction between precisely fabricated surface roughness and applied wettability coatings on two-phase flow regime transitions. A library of test surfaces is created with independently controlled topographical parameters and chemical treatments, enabling the decoupling and subsequent re-coupling of their effects. The effective apparent contact angle and contact angle hysteresis for each combined state are rigorously characterized to define a true surface condition vector. Flow pattern maps and transition criteria are then developed as a function of this coupled surface descriptor, providing a predictive framework valid for realistic, non-ideal surfaces.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Vascular Networks for Target Flow Patterns
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse solution approach from angiogenesis—where vascular networks are optimized to meet metabolic demands—to the design of engineered fluid conduits. The analogous problem in biomedical engineering involves determining the optimal branching geometry of blood vessels (diameters, lengths, angles) to achieve a target pressure or flow distribution, solved using cost minimization (e.g., Murray's Law minimizing pumping power). Adapted to this context, we propose an inverse design framework where a target flow pattern (e.g., uniform wall shear, specific mixing profile) is specified as the 'demand.' An optimization algorithm, minimizing an engineering cost function (like energy dissipation or pressure loss), would then solve for the required tube geometry (including non-uniform cross-sections, curvature, and bifurcations) and operational conditions to maintain that precise pattern, enabling the tailored design of fluidic systems for specific applications.

ideas.title: Study scaling from normal to hypergravity

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Dynamical Mode Decomposition for Predictive Flow Transition Modeling
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond modeling transitions as black-box trajectories by constructing a low-order dynamical system from high-fidelity data or direct numerical simulation. The system's core is a set of evolution equations for the amplitudes of dynamically relevant modes, where the model structure (e.g., quadratic nonlinearity for triad interactions) and parameters are rigorously derived and constrained by the underlying Navier-Stokes equations. This ensures the predicted transient dynamics respect fundamental physics. The final model can then predict not just the path of a transition for a single case, but can be interrogated to understand stability, bifurcations, and the effect of perturbations across a parameter space.

ideas.title: Geostatistical Kriging of Interfacial Drag for High-Fidelity Multiphase Flow Field Reconstruction
ideas.description: This research synthesizes geostatistical kriging with advanced interfacial drag models to develop a framework for the spatial and parametric interpolation of multiphase flow regimes. It treats sparse, pointwise data from experiments or high-fidelity simulations—where local interfacial drag correlations (e.g., for annular/mist flow) are known—as a spatially correlated random field. By applying kriging, the method interpolates both the flow parameters and the appropriate interfacial drag correlation itself across gaps in the parameter space and physical domain, generating a continuous, probabilistic map of the drag field. This enables the high-fidelity reconstruction of complete flow fields with quantified uncertainty, directly informing system-wide modeling in applications like wellbore hydraulics or reactor design where localized flow regime transitions are critical.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Flow Systems via Machine Learning Pattern Recognition and Optimization
ideas.description: This research fuses inverse problem formulation with machine learning classification to create an automated design system for fluidic components. A convolutional neural network is first trained as a high-dimensional classifier, mapping flow visualization data (e.g., dye advection, particle image velocimetry fields) to discrete and continuous pattern descriptors. This trained model then acts as a fast, differentiable surrogate within an optimization algorithm, which iteratively adjusts tube geometry and inlet conditions to minimize the difference between the predicted flow pattern and a desired target pattern. The synergy enables the efficient, goal-oriented design of complex flow systems where the target is defined not by simple performance metrics but by a specific, complex flow structure itself.

ideas.title: Use interferometry to measure thin-film profiles

ideas.title: Adapting Geomorphological Roughness Scaling to Predict Flow Pattern Transitions
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from fluvial geomorphology and hydrology. In that field, the Manning's roughness coefficient and relative scaling concepts are used to predict how channel bed roughness alters flow resistance and regime transitions in open channels. The analogous solution involves correlating a scaled roughness parameter to critical flow numbers. Adapted to the context of internal tube flow, the idea is to develop a composite, dimensionless surface descriptor that integrates both physical roughness amplitude and chemical wettability (contact angle). This descriptor would function as an 'effective relative roughness.' The core research activity would involve systematic experiments to create a new map or correlation showing how this parameter modifies the traditional boundaries between multiphase flow patterns (e.g., bubbly, slug, churn, annular) in a tube, thereby challenging the assumption that smooth, idealized surface conditions are sufficient for predicting transitions.

ideas.title: Predicting Flow Regime Transitions via Metastable State Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the analogous problem of predicting protein folding pathways from biochemistry to the context of flow pattern transitions. In protein folding, Markov State Models (MSMs) are used to model the dynamics between metastable conformational states, predicting transition pathways and kinetics. The adapted idea applies this MSM framework to fluid dynamical systems. By defining coarse-grained flow patterns (e.g., laminar, intermittent, turbulent states) from data and constructing a Markov model of transitions between them, the research aims to quantitatively model the transient dynamics between flow regimes. This approach would predict the most probable transition pathways and time scales, offering a dynamic complement to traditional steady-state flow regime maps.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Hazard Maps for New Geological Contexts to Extend Flow Pattern Maps to New Fluids
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from geophysics, where seismic hazard maps are adapted for new geological contexts. The analogous solution involves using scaling laws and identifying analogous features to translate a model from one region to another. Adapted to the original seed idea, this means treating the established refrigerant flow pattern map as a baseline model. The research would systematically identify the key dimensionless property ratios (e.g., density ratio, viscosity ratio, surface tension) that govern flow regime transitions. By scaling the map's transition boundaries according to these ratios for target fluids like water, ammonia, or nanofluids, a generalized framework can be developed. The adapted map must then be rigorously tested and calibrated against experimental flow visualization data for each new fluid class.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Prediction of Flow Regime Transitions via Spatiotemporal Sequence Learning
ideas.description: This research direction integrates dynamic transition modeling with machine learning classification to develop a predictive tool for flow regime evolution. By training deep learning models, such as recurrent neural networks or transformers, on time-series flow visualization data, the system learns the spatiotemporal signatures that precede a transition. This allows for the prediction of both the timing and the destination flow pattern of an impending regime change. The result is a data-driven early-warning system for flow instabilities, moving beyond static classification to active forecasting of system dynamics.

ideas.title: Hybrid VOF-Porous Media Model for Wavy Films with Dynamically Coupled Substrate Treatment
ideas.description: This research develops a novel computational framework that hybridizes explicit interface tracking for large waves with a porous media treatment for the underlying thin film substrate. The model dynamically partitions the domain: the gas-liquid interface and large wave structures are resolved using the Volume-of-Fluid method, while the thin, quasi-stationary liquid layer beneath is modeled as a porous medium with temporally and spatially varying properties. A two-way coupling scheme continuously updates the porous region's effective permeability and porosity based on the instantaneous shear and pressure from the resolved wave flow, ensuring physical consistency. This approach captures the essential wave dynamics while efficiently modeling the substrate's resistance, overcoming the rigidity constraint of classical porous media models.

ideas.title: Adapting Geological Stratigraphy Mapping to Inclined Flow Pattern Classification
ideas.description: This research seed expands the challenge to the horizontal tube assumption by drawing an analogy from structural geology. In geology, the mapping and analysis of sedimentary strata fundamentally change when layers are inclined rather than horizontal, requiring specialized mapping techniques and trigonometric corrections to underlying physical models (e.g., for fluid flow through porous media). Analogously, this project investigates flow pattern maps for non-horizontal tubes by adapting this geological solution. The core idea is to treat two-phase flow regimes as mutable 'layers' whose transitions and characteristics are functions of the tube's inclination angle. The research will develop a generalized framework where flow pattern map boundaries and predictive correlations are parameterized by inclination (θ), incorporating trigonometric corrections to account for the shifting influence of gravitational (buoyancy) and shear forces, thereby creating a unified model valid for horizontal, inclined, and vertical orientations.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Seismic Tomography for Large-Diameter Pipe Flow Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from geophysical seismic tomography—where arrays of sensors and inversion models map large, inaccessible subsurface structures—to the problem of mapping flow in industrial-scale, large-diameter pipes. The analogous solution involves deploying a dense sensor array around the pipe's exterior or at strategic interior points, using the fluid's inherent dynamic noise or introduced perturbations as the probing signal. By applying computational inversion techniques similar to those in tomography, the collected multi-point data can be reconstructed into a comprehensive, scalable map of the flow field (e.g., velocity profiles, swirl, sediment or bubble distribution), enabling diagnostics and optimization for power plant coolant or feedwater systems without intrusive measurements.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Bayesian Assimilation of Distributed Pressure Signatures for Robust Flow Pattern Identification
ideas.description: This redesigned approach tackles the core inverse problem ambiguity in inferring flow patterns from pressure. It employs a bank of physics-based models, each representing a candidate flow regime, to generate simulated pressure signals. A Bayesian inference framework then assimilates high-order statistical and spectral features from multiple, spatially distributed pressure transducers to compute the posterior probability for each model. This system directly quantifies classification uncertainty and robustly identifies the most probable flow pattern by finding the physical model whose predictions best match the multi-point, multi-scale statistical signature of the measured pressure field.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Self-Supervised Learning for Robust Flow Regime Identification
ideas.description: This redesigned approach employs a hybrid machine learning architecture that leverages both vast amounts of unlabeled sensor data and fundamental physical principles. A self-supervised model first learns generalized representations of flow dynamics through contrastive learning on temporal sensor sequences. These representations are then regularized using a physics-informed loss function that penalizes predictions violating basic conservation laws. This combination allows the system to achieve high classification accuracy and robustness to noise with minimal labeled data, while also identifying novel or transitional regimes not present in the original training set.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Waveform Mapping to Model Flow Patterns in Non-Horizontal Tubes
ideas.description: The original idea seeks to extend a flow-pattern-dependent modeling approach to vertical and inclined tube geometries. Drawing an analogy from geophysics, where solutions for seismic wave propagation in horizontal layers are successfully adapted to inclined and faulted subsurface structures using coordinate transformations and reference frame rotation. The adapted research idea is to apply similar mathematical techniques from seismology to fluid dynamics. By developing a coordinate transformation framework that incorporates the orientation of the gravity vector, the well-characterized flow pattern maps and associated closure relations for horizontal tubes can be systematically mapped and validated for vertical and inclined orientations, providing a generalized modeling approach.

ideas.title: Adapt Optical Flow from Computer Vision for PIV in Two-Phase Flows
ideas.description: This idea proposes adapting the solution to motion estimation from the field of computer vision—specifically, dense optical flow algorithms used for video analysis—to the problem of measuring detailed two-phase velocity profiles with Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV). The analogous solution uses brightness constancy constraints to calculate a dense field of displacement vectors for every pixel between consecutive frames. The adapted research idea is to integrate optical flow methodologies into the PIV processing pipeline. A hybrid algorithm would be developed where a dense optical flow pre-processing step generates an initial velocity field, which is then used to inform and constrain the window-based cross-correlation central to PIV. This adaptation aims to increase the spatial resolution and robustness of velocity measurements in complex two-phase flows, particularly near phase boundaries where traditional PIV can struggle due to contrast variations or uneven particle distribution.

ideas.title: Seismic Stratigraphy for Flow: Inferring Flow Pattern from Pressure Signal Spectral Decomposition
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from seismic stratigraphy, where subsurface geological patterns are inferred from seismic signal spectra, to the problem of inferring two-phase flow patterns from pressure drop measurements. In the analogous field, spectral decomposition of seismic traces reveals frequency-domain signatures characteristic of specific depositional environments. The adapted research idea treats the pressure drop time-series as an acoustic signal from the flow itself. By applying spectral decomposition techniques (like wavelet or Fourier transforms) to this signal, we can extract unique spectral fingerprints—such as dominant frequencies, bandwidth, and energy decay—that correlate with internal flow structures. A machine learning model can then be trained on laboratory data to map these spectral features to known flow patterns, providing a novel, non-intrusive method for flow regime identification using only a pressure transducer.

ideas.title: Non-stationary Process-Convolution Kriging for Flow Regime Prediction Using Covariate-Guided Local Spatial Structure
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the stationarity limitation of classical kriging by developing a hybrid geostatistical-machine learning framework for spatial interpolation of flow regimes. The system first uses relevant spatial covariates to model how the spatial dependence structure (variogram) changes locally across the domain. It then employs a process-convolution or spatially-varying parameter kriging method, where the interpolation weights are adapted point-by-point based on the local learned spatial model. This results in robust predictions within parameter gaps that respect heterogeneous physical controls, significantly improving accuracy in complex, non-stationary environments.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Signal Classification for Flow Regime Recognition
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogous solution from geophysics, where machine learning models classify seismic event types from waveform data, to the problem of flow regime recognition. In seismology, convolutional neural networks are successfully used to automatically identify patterns in raw seismic signals for event classification. Adapted to fluid dynamics, this involves using a similar deep learning architecture to process multi-channel time-series data from flow sensors (like pressure transducers or impedance probes) as input. The model would be trained to recognize the characteristic temporal and spectral signatures of different flow regimes, providing a robust, automated classification tool that learns features directly from the data, improving accuracy and generalizability over traditional threshold-based methods.

ideas.title: Apply Convolutional Neural Networks for Flow Pattern Classification Inspired by Medical Imaging Diagnostics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a proven solution from medical imaging diagnostics to the domain of fluid dynamics. In medical imaging, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are successfully deployed to classify complex visual patterns in scans for disease diagnosis. The analogous solution is to apply this same deep learning architecture to flow visualization data. By training a CNN on expert-labeled images of different flow regimes (such as laminar, turbulent, or specific vortex patterns from particle image velocimetry or schlieren photography), the model can learn to automatically and accurately classify flow patterns. This provides a powerful, automated method for analyzing fluid behavior in experiments and simulations, transforming qualitative visual assessment into a quantitative, rapid classification tool.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Topological Feature Learning for Robust Flow Pattern Characterization
ideas.description: This redesigned approach combines deep learning and topological data analysis within a physics-constrained framework to achieve robust characterization of complex flows. A convolutional autoencoder, trained on high-fidelity simulation data with embedded physical constraints, first extracts denoised, multi-scale representations of coherent structures from raw experimental images. Persistent homology is then applied to these learned feature maps to compute topological signatures. Finally, a regularization step filters the topological descriptors using known physical principles, ensuring the final output captures only the topologically persistent features that are consistent with the underlying fluid dynamics.
